These shifts expose persistent challenges for human-data interactive systems, including perceptually misaligned latency, scalability constraints, limitations of existing interaction and exploration paradigms, and growing uncertainty regarding the reliability and interpretability of AI-generated insights. Responding to these challenges requires moving beyond conventional efficiency and scalability metrics, redefining the roles of humans and machines in analytical workflows, and incorporating cognitive, perceptual, and design principles into every level of the human-data interaction stack. This paper investigates the challenges introduced by recent advances in AI and examines how these developments are reshaping the ways users engage with data, while outlining limitations and open research directions for building human-centered AI systems for interactive data analysis in the AI era.

AI.tldr: AI advancements are transforming human-data interaction systems, introducing challenges with large-scale unstructured data, foundation models, uncertainty, and misaligned latency, requiring new approaches beyond traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: The rapid advancement of AI is fundamentally changing human-centered systems, creating new challenges in human-AI interaction, human-data interaction, and visual analytics due to large-scale heterogeneous data, foundation models, and increased uncertainty in analytical processes.
AI.method: The paper investigates challenges introduced by recent AI advances, examines how these developments reshape user engagement with data, and outlines limitations and open research directions for building human-centered AI systems for interactive data analysis.
AI.result: The analysis identifies persistent challenges including perceptually misaligned latency, scalability constraints, limitations of existing interaction paradigms, and growing uncertainty about AI-generated insights' reliability and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Addressing these challenges requires moving beyond conventional efficiency metrics, redefining human-machine roles in analytical workflows, and incorporating cognitive, perceptual, and design principles throughout the human-data interaction stack to build effective human-centered AI systems.

title: EigenData: A Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Platform for Function-Calling Data Synthesis, Auditing, and Repair
summary: Function-calling agents -- large language models that invoke tools and APIs -- require high-quality, domain-specific training data spanning executable environments, backing databases, and diverse multi-turn trajectories. We introduce EigenData, an integrated, self-evolving platform that automates the full data lifecycle through a multi-agent architecture. A top-level orchestrator, EigenCore, coordinates three specialized sub-systems: DatabaseAgent for realistic domain database construction, CodingAgent for verified executable environment generation with iterative test-debug loops, and DataAgent for multi-turn trajectory synthesis with self-evolving prompt optimization. Cross-component feedback ensures consistency across all artifacts. We apply EigenData to audit and repair the Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL-V3), identifying systematic errors in function schemas, implementations, and reference trajectories, automatically correcting them through coordinated schema refinement, code-level bug fixes, and trajectory modification, and introducing an outcome-aware evaluation protocol that assesses task success via database-state correctness rather than turn-level trajectory matching. We demonstrate that the repaired benchmark, coupled with outcome-aware metrics, produces model rankings substantially better correlated with human judgments of functional correctness.

AI.tldr: EigenData is an automated platform for generating high-quality training data for function-calling agents, featuring a multi-agent architecture that creates realistic databases, executable environments, and multi-turn trajectories with cross-component consistency.
AI.motivation: Function-calling agents require high-quality, domain-specific training data spanning executable environments, backing databases, and diverse multi-turn trajectories, but creating such data manually is challenging and error-prone.
AI.method: EigenData uses a multi-agent architecture with EigenCore orchestrator coordinating three specialized sub-systems: DatabaseAgent for realistic domain database construction, CodingAgent for verified executable environment generation with iterative test-debug loops, and DataAgent for multi-turn trajectory synthesis with self-evolving prompt optimization, all with cross-component feedback for consistency.
AI.result: Applied to audit and repair the Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL-V3), EigenData identified systematic errors in function schemas, implementations, and reference trajectories, automatically corrected them, and introduced outcome-aware evaluation that assesses task success via database-state correctness rather than turn-level trajectory matching.
AI.conclusion: The repaired benchmark with outcome-aware metrics produces model rankings substantially better correlated with human judgments of functional correctness, demonstrating EigenData's effectiveness for generating high-quality function-calling agent training data and improving evaluation protocols.

title: Model Change for Description Logic Concepts
summary: We consider the problem of modifying a description logic concept in light of models represented as pointed interpretations. We call this setting model change, and distinguish three main kinds of changes: eviction, which consists of only removing models; reception, which incorporates models; and revision, which combines removal with incorporation of models in a single operation. We introduce a formal notion of revision and argue that it does not reduce to a simple combination of eviction and reception, contrary to intuition. We provide positive and negative results on the compatibility of eviction and reception for EL and ALC description logic concepts and on the compatibility of revision for ALC concepts.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes model change operations for description logic concepts, distinguishing eviction (removing models), reception (adding models), and revision (combined removal and addition), showing revision doesn't reduce to simple combination of eviction and reception.
AI.motivation: To formalize and analyze operations for modifying description logic concepts based on models represented as pointed interpretations, addressing how concepts can be changed when faced with new model information.
AI.method: Introduces formal notions of eviction, reception, and revision operations for description logic concepts. Analyzes compatibility of these operations for EL and ALC description logics, examining whether revision reduces to combination of eviction and reception.
AI.result: Provides both positive and negative results: shows revision does not reduce to simple combination of eviction and reception contrary to intuition. Presents compatibility results for eviction and reception in EL and ALC, and compatibility results for revision in ALC.
AI.conclusion: Revision is a distinct operation that cannot be decomposed into sequential eviction and reception, requiring separate formal treatment. The analysis provides foundational understanding of model change operations for description logics.

title: When AI Levels the Playing Field: Skill Homogenization, Asset Concentration, and Two Regimes of Inequality
summary: Generative AI compresses within-task skill differences while shifting economic value toward concentrated complementary assets, creating an apparent paradox: the technology that equalizes individual performance may widen aggregate inequality. We formalize this tension in a task-based model with endogenous education, employer screening, and heterogeneous firms. The model yields two regimes whose boundary depends on AI's technology structure (proprietary vs.\ commodity) and labor market institutions (rent-sharing elasticity, asset concentration). A scenario analysis via Method of Simulated Moments, matching six empirical targets, disciplines the model's quantitative magnitudes; a sensitivity decomposition reveals that the five non-$Δ$Gini moments identify mechanism rates but not the aggregate sign, which at the calibrated parameters is pinned by $m_6$ and $ξ$, while AI's technology structure ($η_1$ vs.\ $η_0$) independently crosses the boundary. The contribution is the mechanism -- not a verdict on the sign. Occupation-level regressions using BLS OEWS data (2019--2023) illustrate why such data cannot test the model's task-level predictions. The predictions are testable with within-occupation, within-task panel data that do not yet exist at scale.

AI.tldr: Generative AI equalizes individual task performance but may increase aggregate inequality by shifting economic value to concentrated complementary assets, creating a paradox between micro-level equalization and macro-level inequality.
AI.motivation: To resolve the apparent paradox where generative AI technology that equalizes individual performance differences within tasks may simultaneously widen aggregate economic inequality, by examining how AI shifts economic value toward concentrated complementary assets.
AI.method: Develop a task-based model with endogenous education, employer screening, and heterogeneous firms; conduct scenario analysis via Method of Simulated Moments matching six empirical targets; perform sensitivity decomposition; use occupation-level regressions with BLS OEWS data (2019-2023).
AI.result: The model yields two regimes depending on AI's technology structure (proprietary vs. commodity) and labor market institutions; calibrated parameters show the aggregate inequality sign is determined by specific moments and parameters; occupation-level data cannot test the model's task-level predictions.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is the mechanism explaining the tension between micro-level equalization and macro-level inequality, not a definitive verdict on the sign of inequality effects; predictions require within-occupation, within-task panel data that currently don't exist at scale.

title: Tool-Genesis: A Task-Driven Tool Creation Benchmark for Self-Evolving Language Agent
summary: Research on self-evolving language agents has accelerated, drawing increasing attention to their ability to create, adapt, and maintain tools from task requirements. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on predefined specifications, which limits scalability and hinders truly autonomous evolution. While recent studies attempt to dynamically generate tools, they primarily emphasize downstream performance, resulting in a "black-box" evaluation that makes it difficult to attribute failures to specific causes. To address this, we propose Tool-Genesis, a diagnostic benchmark designed to quantify agent capabilities across multiple dimensions, including interface compliance, functional correctness, and downstream utility. Tool-Genesis evaluates whether agents can construct task-relevant tools solely from abstract requirements (without preset specifications) and use them to solve realistic problems. Crucially, we find that even state-of-the-art models struggle to produce precise tool interfaces or executable logic in a one-shot setting. These minor initial flaws are amplified through the pipeline, leading to a sharp degradation in downstream metrics. We hope Tool-Genesis will guide future research toward training and steering models to synthesize persistent, general-purpose tools that better address real-world challenges.

AI.tldr: Tool-Genesis is a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating self-evolving language agents' ability to create tools from abstract requirements without predefined specifications, focusing on interface compliance, functional correctness, and downstream utility.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for self-evolving language agents rely on predefined specifications, limiting scalability and hindering truly autonomous evolution. Current approaches focus on downstream performance with "black-box" evaluation, making it difficult to attribute failures to specific causes.
AI.method: Proposed Tool-Genesis benchmark quantifies agent capabilities across multiple dimensions: interface compliance, functional correctness, and downstream utility. It evaluates whether agents can construct task-relevant tools solely from abstract requirements (without preset specifications) and use them to solve realistic problems.
AI.result: Even state-of-the-art models struggle to produce precise tool interfaces or executable logic in a one-shot setting. Minor initial flaws are amplified through the pipeline, leading to sharp degradation in downstream metrics.
AI.conclusion: Tool-Genesis aims to guide future research toward training and steering models to synthesize persistent, general-purpose tools that better address real-world challenges, moving beyond black-box evaluation to diagnostic assessment.

title: On the Value of Tokeniser Pretraining in Physics Foundation Models
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures. Workshop paper at ICLR 2026 AI & PDE
summary: We investigate the impact of tokeniser pretraining on the accuracy and efficiency of physics emulation. Modern high-resolution simulations produce vast volumes of data spanning diverse physical regimes and scales. Training foundation models to learn the dynamics underlying such data enables the modelling of complex multiphysics phenomena, especially in data-limited settings. The emerging class of physics foundation models typically aims to learn two tasks jointly: (i) extracting compact representations of high-resolution spatiotemporal data, and (ii) capturing governing physical dynamics. However, learning both tasks from scratch simultaneously can impede the effectiveness of either process. We demonstrate that pretraining the tokeniser with an autoencoding objective prior to training the dynamics model enhances computational efficiency for downstream tasks. Notably, the magnitude of this benefit depends on domain alignment: pretraining on the same physical system as the downstream task yields the largest improvements, while pretraining on other systems provides moderate gains. In-domain pretraining reduces VRMSE by 64% after 10,500 training steps compared to training from scratch. To our knowledge, this is the first systematic investigation of tokeniser pretraining for physics foundation models. We further introduce flexible spatiotemporal compression operations that extend causal convolutions to support runtime-adjustable compression ratios, enabling efficient adaptation to diverse downstream tasks. Our findings provide practical guidance for training efficient physics emulators and highlight the importance of strategic pretraining data selection.

AI.tldr: Tokeniser pretraining with autoencoding objectives before dynamics model training improves physics emulation accuracy and efficiency, with domain-aligned pretraining providing the greatest benefits.
AI.motivation: Physics foundation models need to learn both compact representations of high-resolution spatiotemporal data and governing physical dynamics, but learning both simultaneously from scratch can be suboptimal. The paper investigates whether pretraining the tokeniser separately can improve overall performance.
AI.method: Pretrain tokeniser with autoencoding objective before training dynamics model; introduce flexible spatiotemporal compression operations that extend causal convolutions to support runtime-adjustable compression ratios; systematically evaluate pretraining benefits across different domain alignments.
AI.result: In-domain pretraining reduces VRMSE by 64% after 10,500 training steps compared to training from scratch; pretraining on same physical system yields largest improvements, while pretraining on other systems provides moderate gains; flexible compression operations enable efficient adaptation to diverse downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Tokeniser pretraining significantly enhances physics emulation efficiency, with domain alignment being crucial for optimal performance; provides practical guidance for training efficient physics emulators and highlights importance of strategic pretraining data selection.

title: Post Fusion Bird's Eye View Feature Stabilization for Robust Multimodal 3D Detection
summary:
Camera-LiDAR fusion is widely used in autonomous driving to enable accurate 3D object detection. However, bird's-eye view (BEV) fusion detectors can degrade significantly under domain shift and sensor failures, limiting reliability in real-world deployment. Existing robustness approaches often require modifying the fusion architecture or retraining specialized models, making them difficult to integrate into already deployed systems.
  We propose a Post Fusion Stabilizer (PFS), a lightweight module that operates on intermediate BEV representations of existing detectors and produces a refined feature map for the original detection head. The design stabilizes feature statistics under domain shift, suppresses spatial regions affected by sensor degradation, and adaptively restores weakened cues through residual correction. Designed as a near-identity transformation, PFS preserves performance while improving robustness under diverse camera and LiDAR corruptions. Evaluations on the nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that PFS achieves state-of-the-art results in several failure modes, notably improving camera dropout robustness by +1.2% and low-light performance by +4.4% mAP while maintaining a lightweight footprint of only 3.3 M parameters.

AI.tldr: PFS is a lightweight post-fusion stabilizer module that improves robustness of existing BEV fusion detectors against domain shifts and sensor failures without architectural changes or retraining.
AI.motivation: BEV fusion detectors degrade under domain shift and sensor failures, limiting real-world reliability. Existing robustness approaches require architectural modifications or specialized retraining, making integration into deployed systems difficult.
AI.method: PFS operates on intermediate BEV representations of existing detectors, producing refined feature maps for original detection heads. It stabilizes feature statistics under domain shift, suppresses degraded spatial regions, and adaptively restores weakened cues through residual correction. Designed as near-identity transformation to preserve performance.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on nuScenes benchmark: +1.2% mAP improvement for camera dropout robustness, +4.4% mAP improvement for low-light performance, while maintaining lightweight footprint of only 3.3M parameters.
AI.conclusion: PFS provides an effective, lightweight solution to enhance robustness of existing BEV fusion detectors against sensor failures and domain shifts without requiring architectural modifications or retraining, enabling easier integration into deployed autonomous driving systems.

title: Relational Semantic Reasoning on 3D Scene Graphs for Open World Interactive Object Search
summary: Open-world interactive object search in household environments requires understanding semantic relationships between objects and their surrounding context to guide exploration efficiently. Prior methods either rely on vision-language embeddings similarity, which does not reliably capture task-relevant relational semantics, or large language models (LLMs), which are too slow and costly for real-time deployment. We introduce SCOUT: Scene Graph-Based Exploration with Learned Utility for Open-World Interactive Object Search, a novel method that searches directly over 3D scene graphs by assigning utility scores to rooms, frontiers, and objects using relational exploration heuristics such as room-object containment and object-object co-occurrence. To make this practical without sacrificing open-vocabulary generalization, we propose an offline procedural distillation framework that extracts structured relational knowledge from LLMs into lightweight models for on-robot inference. Furthermore, we present SymSearch, a scalable symbolic benchmark for evaluating semantic reasoning in interactive object search tasks. Extensive evaluations across symbolic and simulation environments show that SCOUT outperforms embedding similarity-based methods and matches LLM-level performance while remaining computationally efficient. Finally, real-world experiments demonstrate effective transfer to physical environments, enabling open-world interactive object search under realistic sensing and navigation constraints.

AI.tldr: SCOUT introduces a scene graph-based exploration method with learned utility for open-world interactive object search, using offline LLM distillation for efficient real-time deployment while maintaining semantic reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current methods for open-world interactive object search have limitations: vision-language embeddings lack reliable relational semantics, while LLMs are too slow and costly for real-time deployment. There's a need for efficient methods that can understand semantic relationships between objects and their context for effective exploration.
AI.method: SCOUT searches directly over 3D scene graphs by assigning utility scores to rooms, frontiers, and objects using relational exploration heuristics (room-object containment, object-object co-occurrence). It uses an offline procedural distillation framework to extract structured relational knowledge from LLMs into lightweight models for on-robot inference. Also introduces SymSearch benchmark for evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show SCOUT outperforms embedding similarity-based methods and matches LLM-level performance while remaining computationally efficient. Real-world experiments demonstrate effective transfer to physical environments under realistic sensing and navigation constraints.
AI.conclusion: SCOUT provides an effective solution for open-world interactive object search by combining scene graph-based exploration with learned utility, achieving semantic reasoning capabilities comparable to LLMs but with computational efficiency suitable for real-time robotic deployment.

title: The Fragility Of Moral Judgment In Large Language Models
summary:
People increasingly use large language models (LLMs) for everyday moral and interpersonal guidance, yet these systems cannot interrogate missing context and judge dilemmas as presented. We introduce a perturbation framework for testing the stability and manipulability of LLM moral judgments while holding the underlying moral conflict constant. Using 2,939 dilemmas from r/AmItheAsshole (January-March 2025), we generate three families of content perturbations: surface edits (lexical/structural noise), point-of-view shifts (voice and stance neutralization), and persuasion cues (self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, victim framing). We also vary the evaluation protocol (output ordering, instruction placement, and unstructured prompting). We evaluated all variants with four models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5-72B) (N=129,156 judgments).
  Surface perturbations produce low flip rates (7.5%), largely within the self-consistency noise floor (4-13%), whereas point-of-view shifts induce substantially higher instability (24.3%). A large subset of dilemmas (37.9%) is robust to surface noise yet flips under perspective changes, indicating that models condition on narrative voice as a pragmatic cue. Instability concentrates in morally ambiguous cases; scenarios where no party is assigned blame are most susceptible. Persuasion perturbations yield systematic directional shifts. Protocol choices dominate all other factors: agreement between structured protocols is only 67.6% (kappa=0.55), and only 35.7% of model-scenario units match across all three protocols. These results show that LLM moral judgments are co-produced by narrative form and task scaffolding, raising reproducibility and equity concerns when outcomes depend on presentation skill rather than moral substance.

AI.tldr: LLM moral judgments are highly sensitive to narrative framing and evaluation protocols, with perspective shifts causing 24.3% flip rates and protocol choices dominating all other factors, raising reproducibility and equity concerns.
AI.motivation: People increasingly use LLMs for moral guidance, but these systems cannot interrogate missing context or judge dilemmas as presented, necessitating testing of their judgment stability and manipulability.
AI.method: Introduced a perturbation framework using 2,939 dilemmas from r/AmItheAsshole (Jan-Mar 2025) with three perturbation families: surface edits (lexical/structural noise), point-of-view shifts (voice/stance neutralization), and persuasion cues (self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, victim framing). Varied evaluation protocols (output ordering, instruction placement, unstructured prompting) and tested four models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5-72B) for N=129,156 judgments.
AI.result: Surface perturbations produced low flip rates (7.5%), within self-consistency noise floor. Point-of-view shifts induced substantially higher instability (24.3%). 37.9% of dilemmas robust to surface noise but flipped under perspective changes. Instability concentrated in morally ambiguous cases. Persuasion perturbations yielded systematic directional shifts. Protocol choices dominated all factors: agreement between structured protocols only 67.6% (kappa=0.55), and only 35.7% of model-scenario units matched across all three protocols.
AI.conclusion: LLM moral judgments are co-produced by narrative form and task scaffolding, raising reproducibility and equity concerns when outcomes depend on presentation skill rather than moral substance.

title: When Rubrics Fail: Error Enumeration as Reward in Reference-Free RL Post-Training for Virtual Try-On
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and Rubrics as Rewards (RaR) have driven strong gains in domains with clear correctness signals and even in subjective domains by synthesizing evaluation criteria from ideal reference answers. But many real-world tasks admit multiple valid outputs and lack the single ideal answer that rubric generation depends on. We identify this reference-free setting as a gap in current post-training methods and propose Implicit Error Counting (IEC) to fill it. Instead of checking what a response gets right against a rubric, IEC enumerates what it gets wrong, applying severity-weighted scores across task-relevant axes and converting them into calibrated per-aspect rewards. We show that naïve explicit enumeration is too noisy for stable optimization, and that two design choices: implicit score emission and group calibration are necessary to make error counting a reliable reward. As a case study, we validate IEC on virtual try-on (VTO), a domain that is simultaneously too constrained for holistic scoring and too permissive for rubric-based evaluation: subtle garment errors are unacceptable, yet many output variations are correct. We introduce Cascaded Error Counting (CEC) as an evaluation metric, which tracks human preferences well (60% top-1 vs. 30% others), and curate Mismatch-DressCode (MDressBench), a benchmark with maximal attribute mismatch to stress-test reward designs. On MDressBench, IEC outperforms RaR across all metrics (CEC: 5.31 vs. 5.60 on flat references; 5.20 vs. 5.53 on non-flat). On VITON-HD and DressCode, IEC matches or surpasses six baselines on 6 of 8 perceptual metrics. These results suggest that when ideal answers are unavailable, counting errors provide a stronger signal than constructing rubrics.

AI.tldr: Implicit Error Counting (IEC) addresses the reference-free RL problem by enumerating what responses get wrong rather than checking what they get right, using severity-weighted scores and calibration to create reliable rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR and RaR methods require ideal reference answers for rubric generation, but many real-world tasks lack single ideal answers and admit multiple valid outputs, creating a gap in post-training methods.
AI.method: IEC enumerates what responses get wrong rather than checking what they get right, applying severity-weighted scores across task-relevant axes and converting them into calibrated per-aspect rewards. Two key design choices are implicit score emission and group calibration to stabilize optimization. Cascaded Error Counting (CEC) is introduced as an evaluation metric.
AI.result: IEC outperforms RaR on MDressBench (CEC: 5.31 vs. 5.60 on flat references; 5.20 vs. 5.53 on non-flat). On VITON-HD and DressCode, IEC matches or surpasses six baselines on 6 of 8 perceptual metrics. CEC tracks human preferences well (60% top-1 vs. 30% others).
AI.conclusion: When ideal answers are unavailable, counting errors provides a stronger signal than constructing rubrics. IEC successfully addresses the reference-free setting gap in post-training methods.

title: SecureRAG-RTL: A Retrieval-Augmented, Multi-Agent, Zero-Shot LLM-Driven Framework for Hardware Vulnerability Detection
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks, yet their application in hardware security verification remains limited due to scarcity of publicly available hardware description language (HDL) datasets. This knowledge gap constrains LLM performance in detecting vulnerabilities within HDL designs. To address this challenge, we propose SecureRAG-RTL, a novel Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based approach that significantly enhances LLM-based security verification of hardware designs. Our approach integrates domain-specific retrieval with generative reasoning, enabling models to overcome inherent limitations in hardware security expertise. We establish baseline vulnerability detection rates using prompt-only methods and then demonstrate that SecureRAG-RTL achieves substantial improvements across diverse LLM architectures, regardless of size. On average, our method increases detection accuracy by about 30%, highlighting its effectiveness in bridging domain knowledge gaps. For evaluation, we curated and annotated a benchmark dataset of 14 HDL designs containing real-world security vulnerabilities, which we will release publicly to support future research. These findings underscore the potential of RAG-driven augmentation to enable scalable, efficient, and accurate hardware security verification workflows.

AI.tldr: SecureRAG-RTL enhances hardware security verification by combining retrieval-augmented generation with LLMs, achieving ~30% accuracy improvement over baseline methods on a curated HDL vulnerability dataset.
AI.motivation: LLMs have limited application in hardware security verification due to scarcity of publicly available HDL datasets, creating a knowledge gap that constrains vulnerability detection in hardware designs.
AI.method: Proposes SecureRAG-RTL, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based approach that integrates domain-specific retrieval with generative reasoning to overcome hardware security expertise limitations in LLMs.
AI.result: Achieves ~30% average improvement in vulnerability detection accuracy across diverse LLM architectures compared to prompt-only baseline methods, regardless of model size.
AI.conclusion: RAG-driven augmentation enables scalable, efficient, and accurate hardware security verification workflows, with the release of a benchmark dataset of 14 HDL designs containing real-world vulnerabilities to support future research.

title: Longitudinal Lesion Inpainting in Brain MRI via 3D Region Aware Diffusion
summary: Accurate longitudinal analysis of brain MRI is often hindered by evolving lesions, which bias automated neuroimaging pipelines. While deep generative models have shown promise in inpainting these lesions, most existing methods operate cross-sectionally or lack 3D anatomical continuity. We present a novel pseudo-3D longitudinal inpainting framework based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). Our approach utilizes multi-channel conditioning to incorporate longitudinal context from distinct visits (t_1, t_2) and extends Region-Aware Diffusion (RAD) to the medical domain, focusing the generative process on pathological regions without altering surrounding healthy tissue. We evaluated our model against state-of-the-art baselines on longitudinal brain MRI from 93 patients. Our model significantly outperforms the leading baseline (FastSurfer-LIT) in terms of perceptual fidelity, reducing the Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) distance from 0.07 to 0.03 while effectively eliminating inter-slice discontinuities. Furthermore, our model demonstrates high longitudinal stability with a Temporal Fidelity Index of 1.024, closely approaching the ideal value of 1.0 and substantially narrowing the gap compared to LIT's TFI of 1.22. Notably, the RAD mechanism provides a substantial gain in efficiency; our framework achieves an average processing time of 2.53 min per volume, representing approximately 10x speedup over the 24.30 min required by LIT. By leveraging longitudinal priors and region-specific denoising, our framework provides a highly reliable and efficient preprocessing step for the study of progressive neurodegenerative diseases. A derivative dataset consisting of 93 pre-processed scans used for testing will be available upon request after acceptance. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Novel pseudo-3D longitudinal inpainting framework using Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) with multi-channel conditioning and Region-Aware Diffusion (RAD) for brain MRI lesion repair, achieving superior perceptual fidelity, temporal stability, and 10x speedup over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Accurate longitudinal brain MRI analysis is hindered by evolving lesions that bias automated neuroimaging pipelines. Existing deep generative models for lesion inpainting either operate cross-sectionally or lack 3D anatomical continuity, limiting their effectiveness for longitudinal studies of progressive neurodegenerative diseases.
AI.method: Pseudo-3D longitudinal inpainting framework based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) with multi-channel conditioning incorporating longitudinal context from distinct visits (t₁, t₂). Extends Region-Aware Diffusion (RAD) to medical domain, focusing generative process on pathological regions without altering surrounding healthy tissue.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms leading baseline (FastSurfer-LIT): reduces LPIPS distance from 0.07 to 0.03, eliminates inter-slice discontinuities, achieves high longitudinal stability (Temporal Fidelity Index of 1.024 vs ideal 1.0, compared to LIT's 1.22). RAD mechanism provides 10x speedup (2.53 min vs 24.30 min per volume). Evaluated on longitudinal brain MRI from 93 patients.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a highly reliable and efficient preprocessing step for studying progressive neurodegenerative diseases by leveraging longitudinal priors and region-specific denoising. Code and derivative dataset of 93 pre-processed scans will be released upon acceptance.

title: Autonomous Algorithm Discovery for Ptychography via Evolutionary LLM Reasoning
summary: Ptychography is a computational imaging technique widely used for high-resolution materials characterization, but high-quality reconstructions often require the use of regularization functions that largely remain manually designed. We introduce Ptychi-Evolve, an autonomous framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to discover and evolve novel regularization algorithms. The framework combines LLM-driven code generation with evolutionary mechanisms, including semantically-guided crossover and mutation. Experiments on three challenging datasets (X-ray integrated circuits, low-dose electron microscopy of apoferritin, and multislice imaging with crosstalk artifacts) demonstrate that discovered regularizers outperform conventional reconstructions, achieving up to +0.26 SSIM and +8.3~dB PSNR improvements. Besides, Ptychi-Evolve records algorithm lineage and evolution metadata, enabling interpretable and reproducible analysis of discovered regularizers.

AI.tldr: Ptychi-Evolve: LLM-driven evolutionary framework autonomously discovers novel regularization algorithms for ptychography, outperforming conventional methods across multiple challenging imaging datasets.
AI.motivation: Ptychography requires manually designed regularization functions for high-quality reconstructions, creating a bottleneck in computational imaging. The paper aims to automate this process by leveraging LLMs to discover and evolve novel regularization algorithms autonomously.
AI.method: Ptychi-Evolve combines LLM-driven code generation with evolutionary mechanisms including semantically-guided crossover and mutation. The framework autonomously generates, evaluates, and evolves regularization algorithms for ptychographic reconstruction.
AI.result: Experiments on three challenging datasets (X-ray integrated circuits, low-dose electron microscopy of apoferritin, and multislice imaging with crosstalk artifacts) show discovered regularizers outperform conventional reconstructions, achieving up to +0.26 SSIM and +8.3 dB PSNR improvements.
AI.conclusion: Ptychi-Evolve successfully automates regularization discovery for ptychography using LLMs and evolutionary algorithms, providing interpretable and reproducible analysis through algorithm lineage tracking while achieving superior reconstruction quality.

title: The Rise of AI in Weather and Climate Information and its Impact on Global Inequality
summary: The rapid adoption of AI in Earth system science promises unprecedented speed and fidelity in the generation of climate information. However, this technological prowess rests on a fragile and unequal foundation: the current trajectory of AI development risks further automating and amplifying the North-South divide in the global climate information system. We outline the global asymmetry in High-Performance Computing and data infrastructure, demonstrating that the development of foundation models is almost exclusively concentrated in the Global North. Using three different domains, we show how this infrastructure inequality continues through models' inputs, processes and outputs. As an example, in weather and climate modelling, the reliance on historically biased data leads to systematic performance gaps that disproportionately affect the most vulnerable regions. In climate impact modelling, data sparsity and unrepresentative validation risk driving misleading interventions and maladaptation. Finally, in large language models, dependence on dominant textualised forms of climate knowledge risks reinforcing existing biases. We conclude that addressing these disparities demands revisiting the three phases, i.e. models Input, Process and Output. This involves (i) a perspective shift from model-centric to data-centric development, (ii) the establishment of a Climate Digital Public Infrastructure and human-centric evaluation metrics, and (iii) a move from producer-consumer dynamics toward knowledge co-production. This integration of diverse knowledge systems would truly democratise compute sovereignty and ensure that the AI revolution fosters genuine systemic resilience rather than exacerbating inequity.

AI.tldr: AI in Earth system science risks amplifying North-South divide due to infrastructure inequality, biased data, and knowledge representation issues, requiring data-centric approaches and knowledge co-production for democratization.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the risk that rapid AI adoption in Earth system science could exacerbate global inequalities by automating and amplifying the North-South divide in climate information systems, despite promising unprecedented speed and fidelity.
AI.method: The authors analyze global asymmetries in High-Performance Computing and data infrastructure, examining three domains: weather/climate modeling (biased historical data), climate impact modeling (data sparsity and validation issues), and large language models (textual knowledge biases). They propose addressing disparities through three phases: Input, Process, and Output.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that foundation model development is concentrated in the Global North, leading to systematic performance gaps in vulnerable regions, misleading interventions from unrepresentative validation, and reinforcement of existing biases through dominant textual knowledge forms.
AI.conclusion: Addressing disparities requires: (i) shifting from model-centric to data-centric development, (ii) establishing Climate Digital Public Infrastructure with human-centric metrics, and (iii) moving from producer-consumer dynamics to knowledge co-production. This integration of diverse knowledge systems would democratize compute sovereignty and ensure AI fosters systemic resilience rather than exacerbating inequity.

title: Cultural Perspectives and Expectations for Generative AI: A Global Survey Approach
summary: There is a lack of empirical evidence about global attitudes around whether and how GenAI should represent cultures. This paper assesses understandings and beliefs about culture as it relates to GenAI from a large-scale global survey. We gathered data about what culture means to different groups, and about how GenAI should approach the representation of cultural artifacts, concepts, or values. We distill working definitions of culture directly from these communities to build an understanding of its conceptual complexities and how they relate to representations in Generative AI. We survey from across parts of Europe, North and South America, Asia, and Africa. We conclude with a set of recommendations for Culture and GenAI development. These include participatory approaches, prioritizing specific cultural dimensions beyond geography, such as religion and tradition, and a sensitivity framework for addressing cultural ``redlines''.

AI.tldr: Global survey reveals diverse cultural definitions and preferences for GenAI representation, leading to recommendations for participatory approaches and sensitivity frameworks.
AI.motivation: Addressing the empirical gap in understanding global attitudes about how Generative AI should represent cultures, given the increasing role of AI in cultural production and representation.
AI.method: Large-scale global survey across Europe, North/South America, Asia, and Africa to gather data on cultural definitions and preferences for GenAI representation of cultural artifacts, concepts, and values.
AI.result: Distilled working definitions of culture from diverse communities, revealing conceptual complexities and specific cultural dimensions (religion, tradition) that should be prioritized beyond geography.
AI.conclusion: Recommends participatory approaches in GenAI development, prioritizing specific cultural dimensions, and implementing sensitivity frameworks to address cultural "redlines" in AI representation.

title: LTLGuard: Formalizing LTL Specifications with Compact Language Models and Lightweight Symbolic Reasoning
summary: Translating informal requirements into formal specifications is challenging due to the ambiguity and variability of natural language (NL). This challenge is particularly pronounced when relying on compact (small and medium) language models, which may lack robust knowledge of temporal logic and thus struggle to produce syntactically valid and consistent formal specifications. In this work, we focus on enabling resource-efficient open-weight models (4B--14B parameters) to generate correct linear temporal logic (LTL) specifications from informal requirements. We present LTLGuard, a modular toolchain that combines constrained generation with formal consistency checking to generate conflict-free LTL specifications from informal input. Our method integrates the generative capabilities of model languages with lightweight automated reasoning tools to iteratively refine candidate specifications, understand the origin of the conflicts and thus help in eliminating inconsistencies. We demonstrate the usability and the effectiveness of our approach and perform quantitative evaluation of the resulting framework.

AI.tldr: LTLGuard enables resource-efficient language models (4B-14B parameters) to generate correct Linear Temporal Logic specifications from informal requirements by combining constrained generation with formal consistency checking.
AI.motivation: Translating informal requirements to formal specifications is challenging due to natural language ambiguity, especially for compact language models that lack robust temporal logic knowledge and struggle to produce syntactically valid, consistent formal specifications.
AI.method: LTLGuard is a modular toolchain combining constrained generation with formal consistency checking to generate conflict-free LTL specifications. It integrates generative capabilities of language models with lightweight automated reasoning tools to iteratively refine candidate specifications and eliminate inconsistencies.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates usability and effectiveness through quantitative evaluation of the resulting framework, showing that resource-efficient open-weight models can generate correct LTL specifications from informal input.
AI.conclusion: LTLGuard successfully enables compact language models to generate correct LTL specifications by leveraging constrained generation and formal consistency checking, addressing the challenge of translating ambiguous natural language requirements into formal specifications.

title: Revisiting the (Sub)Optimality of Best-of-N for Inference-Time Alignment
summary: Best-of-N (BoN) sampling is a widely used inference-time alignment method for language models, whereby N candidate responses are sampled from a reference model and the one with the highest predicted reward according to a learned reward model is selected. Despite its widespread practical use, recent theoretical work has suggested that it is statistically suboptimal and vulnerable to reward hacking, the process by which models exploit weaknesses in the learned reward model to achieve high estimated reward without genuinely improving performance. We revisit this question under assumptions that more closely reflect practice than that of prior work. In particular, in contradistinction to earlier analyses that focused on expected true reward, which may not be meaningful in many practical settings, we investigate how inference-time alignment affects the win-rate, a pairwise comparison-based metric more closely aligned with how reward models are trained and evaluated in practice. We demonstrate that, under minimal conditions on the quality of the reference model and learned reward model, properly tuned BoN is both computationally and statistically optimal in achieving high win-rate, partially explaining its widespread practical success. Because BoN remains susceptible to reward-hacking in this setting, we propose a simple and practical variant that provably eliminates reward-hacking while maintaining optimal statistical performance. Finally, we show that prior approaches are provably suboptimal when considering win-rate, highlighting the importance of choosing appropriate objectives when analyzing inference-time alignment methods.

AI.tldr: BoN sampling is statistically optimal for win-rate objectives (unlike prior analyses focusing on expected reward), but vulnerable to reward hacking; a simple variant eliminates hacking while maintaining optimality.
AI.motivation: Prior theoretical work suggests BoN sampling is statistically suboptimal and vulnerable to reward hacking, but these analyses focus on expected true reward which doesn't align with practical reward model training/evaluation using pairwise comparisons (win-rate).
AI.method: Re-analyze BoN under more practical assumptions focusing on win-rate (pairwise comparison metric). Derive optimality conditions for BoN with proper tuning, then propose a simple variant that eliminates reward hacking while maintaining statistical optimality.
AI.result: Under minimal conditions on reference model and learned reward model quality, properly tuned BoN is computationally and statistically optimal for achieving high win-rate, explaining its practical success. The proposed variant provably eliminates reward hacking while maintaining optimal statistical performance.
AI.conclusion: BoN's practical success is explained by its optimality for win-rate objectives (not expected reward), but reward hacking remains a concern; a simple variant solves this while preserving optimality, highlighting the importance of appropriate objective selection when analyzing inference-time alignment methods.

title: TML-Bench: Benchmark for Data Science Agents on Tabular ML Tasks
summary: Autonomous coding agents can produce strong tabular baselines quickly on Kaggle-style tasks. Practical value depends on end-to-end correctness and reliability under time limits. This paper introduces TML-Bench, a tabular benchmark for data science agents on Kaggle-style tasks. This paper evaluates 10 OSS LLMs on four Kaggle competitions and three time budgets (240s, 600s, and 1200s). Each model is run five times per task and budget. A run is successful if it produces a valid submission and a private-holdout score on hidden labels that are not accessible to the agent. This paper reports median performance, success rates, and run-to-run variability. MiniMax-M2.1 model achieves the best aggregate performance score on all four competitions under the paper's primary aggregation. Average performance improves with larger time budgets. Scaling is noisy for some individual models at the current run count. Code and materials are available at https://github.com/MykolaPinchuk/TML-bench/tree/master.

AI.tldr: TML-Bench is a tabular benchmark for evaluating data science agents on Kaggle-style tasks, testing 10 OSS LLMs across four competitions with varying time budgets, measuring success rates and performance variability.
AI.motivation: To assess the practical value of autonomous coding agents for tabular data science tasks, focusing on end-to-end correctness and reliability under time constraints in Kaggle-style competitions.
AI.method: Introduces TML-Bench benchmark evaluating 10 open-source LLMs on four Kaggle competitions with three time budgets (240s, 600s, 1200s). Each model runs five times per task and budget, with success defined as producing valid submissions and private-holdout scores on hidden labels.
AI.result: MiniMax-M2.1 achieves best aggregate performance across all four competitions. Average performance improves with larger time budgets, though scaling is noisy for some models at current run counts. Reports median performance, success rates, and run-to-run variability.
AI.conclusion: TML-Bench provides a standardized evaluation framework for tabular data science agents, demonstrating that autonomous coding agents can produce strong baselines but performance varies with time budgets and models, with MiniMax-M2.1 showing best overall performance.

title: Bridging Domains through Subspace-Aware Model Merging
comment: Accepted at the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026 (Main Track)
summary: Model merging integrates multiple task-specific models into a single consolidated one. Recent research has made progress in improving merging performance for in-distribution or multi-task scenarios, but domain generalization in model merging remains underexplored. We investigate how merging models fine-tuned on distinct domains affects generalization to unseen domains. Through an analysis of parameter competition in the task matrix using singular value decomposition, we show that merging models trained under different distribution shifts induces stronger conflicts between their subspaces compared to traditional multi-task settings. To mitigate this issue, we propose SCORE (Subspace COnflict-Resolving mErging), a method designed to alleviate such singular subspace conflicts. SCORE finds a shared orthogonal basis by computing the principal components of the concatenated leading singular vectors of all models. It then projects each task matrix into the shared basis, pruning off-diagonal components to remove conflicting singular directions. SCORE consistently outperforms, on average, existing model merging approaches in domain generalization settings across a variety of architectures and model scales, demonstrating its effectiveness and scalability.

AI.tldr: SCORE is a model merging method that addresses domain generalization by resolving subspace conflicts between models fine-tuned on different domains, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Domain generalization in model merging remains underexplored, with existing methods focusing on in-distribution or multi-task scenarios. The paper investigates how merging models fine-tuned on distinct domains affects generalization to unseen domains, identifying that such merging induces stronger subspace conflicts compared to traditional multi-task settings.
AI.method: SCORE (Subspace COnflict-Resolving mErging) addresses subspace conflicts by: 1) Computing principal components of concatenated leading singular vectors from all models to find a shared orthogonal basis, 2) Projecting each task matrix into this shared basis, and 3) Pruning off-diagonal components to remove conflicting singular directions.
AI.result: SCORE consistently outperforms existing model merging approaches in domain generalization settings across various architectures and model scales, demonstrating both effectiveness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the underexplored problem of domain generalization in model merging by identifying subspace conflicts as a key challenge and proposing SCORE as an effective solution that outperforms existing methods.

title: Depth Charge: Jailbreak Large Language Models from Deep Safety Attention Heads
summary: Currently, open-sourced large language models (OSLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable generative performance. However, as their structure and weights are made public, they are exposed to jailbreak attacks even after alignment. Existing attacks operate primarily at shallow levels, such as the prompt or embedding level, and often fail to expose vulnerabilities rooted in deeper model components, which creates a false sense of security for successful defense. In this paper, we propose \textbf{\underline{S}}afety \textbf{\underline{A}}ttention \textbf{\underline{H}}ead \textbf{\underline{A}}ttack (\textbf{SAHA}), an attention-head-level jailbreak framework that explores the vulnerability in deeper but insufficiently aligned attention heads. SAHA contains two novel designs. Firstly, we reveal that deeper attention layers introduce more vulnerability against jailbreak attacks. Based on this finding, \textbf{SAHA} introduces \textit{Ablation-Impact Ranking} head selection strategy to effectively locate the most vital layer for unsafe output. Secondly, we introduce a boundary-aware perturbation method, \textit{i.e. Layer-Wise Perturbation}, to probe the generation of unsafe content with minimal perturbation to the attention. This constrained perturbation guarantees higher semantic relevance with the target intent while ensuring evasion. Extensive experiments show the superiority of our method: SAHA improves ASR by 14\% over SOTA baselines, revealing the vulnerability of the attack surface on the attention head. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SAHA.

AI.tldr: SAHA is an attention-head-level jailbreak attack framework that targets deeper, insufficiently aligned attention heads in open-sourced LLMs to expose vulnerabilities beyond shallow prompt-level attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing jailbreak attacks on open-sourced LLMs operate at shallow levels (prompt/embedding) and fail to expose vulnerabilities in deeper model components, creating a false sense of security. Deeper attention layers contain insufficiently aligned heads that introduce more vulnerability.
AI.method: SAHA uses two novel designs: 1) Ablation-Impact Ranking head selection strategy to locate the most vital layer for unsafe output, and 2) Layer-Wise Perturbation method to probe unsafe content generation with minimal attention perturbation while maintaining semantic relevance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SAHA improves Attack Success Rate (ASR) by 14% over state-of-the-art baselines, revealing vulnerability of the attack surface at the attention head level.
AI.conclusion: SAHA demonstrates that deeper attention heads in OSLLMs contain significant vulnerabilities that shallow attacks miss, providing a more comprehensive framework for evaluating model safety and exposing false security in existing defenses.

title: Knowing without Acting: The Disentangled Geometry of Safety Mechanisms in Large Language Models
summary: Safety alignment is often conceptualized as a monolithic process wherein harmfulness detection automatically triggers refusal. However, the persistence of jailbreak attacks suggests a fundamental mechanistic decoupling. We propose the \textbf{\underline{D}}isentangled \textbf{\underline{S}}afety \textbf{\underline{H}}ypothesis \textbf{(DSH)}, positing that safety computation operates on two distinct subspaces: a \textit{Recognition Axis} ($\mathbf{v}_H$, ``Knowing'') and an \textit{Execution Axis} ($\mathbf{v}_R$, ``Acting''). Our geometric analysis reveals a universal ``Reflex-to-Dissociation'' evolution, where these signals transition from antagonistic entanglement in early layers to structural independence in deep layers. To validate this, we introduce \textit{Double-Difference Extraction} and \textit{Adaptive Causal Steering}. Using our curated \textsc{AmbiguityBench}, we demonstrate a causal double dissociation, effectively creating a state of ``Knowing without Acting.'' Crucially, we leverage this disentanglement to propose the \textbf{Refusal Erasure Attack (REA)}, which achieves State-of-the-Art attack success rates by surgically lobotomizing the refusal mechanism. Furthermore, we uncover a critical architectural divergence, contrasting the \textit{Explicit Semantic Control} of Llama3.1 with the \textit{Latent Distributed Control} of Qwen2.5. The code and dataset are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DSH.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Disentangled Safety Hypothesis (DSH), which posits that safety computation operates on two distinct subspaces: a Recognition Axis ("Knowing") and an Execution Axis ("Acting"). The authors demonstrate a "Reflex-to-Dissociation" evolution where these signals transition from entanglement to independence, enabling a "Knowing without Acting" state and a novel Refusal Erasure Attack (REA) that achieves state-of-the-art jailbreak success rates.
AI.motivation: Current safety alignment is conceptualized as monolithic where harmfulness detection automatically triggers refusal, but persistent jailbreak attacks suggest a fundamental mechanistic decoupling. The authors aim to understand the underlying computational mechanisms of safety in LLMs and explain why jailbreaks remain effective despite safety training.
AI.method: The authors propose the Disentangled Safety Hypothesis (DSH) with Recognition and Execution axes. They introduce Double-Difference Extraction and Adaptive Causal Steering methods, and create AmbiguityBench dataset. They validate the hypothesis through geometric analysis showing "Reflex-to-Dissociation" evolution, and demonstrate causal double dissociation to create "Knowing without Acting" states. They then develop the Refusal Erasure Attack (REA) that surgically targets the refusal mechanism.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates a universal "Reflex-to-Dissociation" evolution where safety signals transition from antagonistic entanglement in early layers to structural independence in deep layers. They achieve causal double dissociation, creating "Knowing without Acting" states. The Refusal Erasure Attack (REA) achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates by lobotomizing the refusal mechanism. They also uncover architectural divergence between Llama3.1's Explicit Semantic Control and Qwen2.5's Latent Distributed Control.
AI.conclusion: Safety computation in LLMs operates on two disentangled subspaces (Recognition and Execution), explaining the persistence of jailbreak attacks. The "Reflex-to-Dissociation" evolution reveals fundamental mechanistic decoupling, enabling novel attacks like REA. This understanding provides insights into safety vulnerabilities and suggests architectural differences in how different models implement safety controls.

title: PVminerLLM: Structured Extraction of Patient Voice from Patient-Generated Text using Large Language Models
summary:
Motivation: Patient-generated text contains critical information about patients' lived experiences, social circumstances, and engagement in care, including factors that strongly influence adherence, care coordination, and health equity. However, these patient voice signals are rarely available in structured form, limiting their use in patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement. Reliable extraction of such information is therefore essential for understanding and addressing non-clinical drivers of health outcomes at scale.
  Results: We introduce PVminer, a benchmark for structured extraction of patient voice, and propose PVminerLLM, a supervised fine-tuned large language model tailored to this task. Across multiple datasets and model sizes, PVminerLLM substantially outperforms prompt-based baselines, achieving up to 83.82% F1 for Code prediction, 80.74% F1 for Sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 for evidence Span extraction. Notably, strong performance is achieved even with smaller models, demonstrating that reliable patient voice extraction is feasible without extreme model scale. These results enable scalable analysis of social and experiential signals embedded in patient-generated text.
  Availability and Implementation: Code, evaluation scripts, and trained LLMs will be released publicly. Annotated datasets will be made available upon request for research use.
  Keywords: Large Language Models, Supervised Fine-Tuning, Medical Annotation, Patient-Generated Text, Clinical NLP

AI.tldr: PVminer introduces a benchmark and fine-tuned LLM (PVminerLLM) for structured extraction of patient voice from clinical text, achieving strong performance on code, sub-code, and evidence span extraction tasks.
AI.motivation: Patient-generated text contains critical information about lived experiences, social circumstances, and care engagement that strongly influences adherence, care coordination, and health equity. These signals are rarely available in structured form, limiting their use in patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement.
AI.method: Proposes PVminer benchmark for structured extraction of patient voice and PVminerLLM, a supervised fine-tuned large language model tailored to this task. Evaluated across multiple datasets and model sizes.
AI.result: PVminerLLM substantially outperforms prompt-based baselines, achieving up to 83.82% F1 for Code prediction, 80.74% F1 for Sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 for evidence Span extraction. Strong performance achieved even with smaller models, demonstrating feasibility without extreme model scale.
AI.conclusion: Reliable patient voice extraction is feasible without extreme model scale, enabling scalable analysis of social and experiential signals embedded in patient-generated text for improved patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement.

title: Visual Words Meet BM25: Sparse Auto-Encoder Visual Word Scoring for Image Retrieval
summary: Dense image retrieval is accurate but offers limited interpretability and attribution, and it can be compute-intensive at scale. We present \textbf{BM25-V}, which applies Okapi BM25 scoring to sparse visual-word activations from a Sparse Auto-Encoder (SAE) on Vision Transformer patch features. Across a large gallery, visual-word document frequencies are highly imbalanced and follow a Zipfian-like distribution, making BM25's inverse document frequency (IDF) weighting well suited for suppressing ubiquitous, low-information words and emphasizing rare, discriminative ones. BM25-V retrieves high-recall candidates via sparse inverted-index operations and serves as an efficient first-stage retriever for dense reranking. Across seven benchmarks, BM25-V achieves Recall@200 $\geq$ 0.993, enabling a two-stage pipeline that reranks only $K{=}200$ candidates per query and recovers near-dense accuracy within $0.2$\% on average. An SAE trained once on ImageNet-1K transfers zero-shot to seven fine-grained benchmarks without fine-tuning, and BM25-V retrieval decisions are attributable to specific visual words with quantified IDF contributions.

AI.tldr: BM25-V applies BM25 scoring to sparse visual-word activations from a Vision Transformer SAE for efficient, high-recall image retrieval with attribution capabilities.
AI.motivation: Dense image retrieval has limitations in interpretability, attribution, and computational efficiency at scale, motivating a sparse retrieval approach.
AI.method: Uses Sparse Auto-Encoder on Vision Transformer patch features to generate visual-word activations, then applies Okapi BM25 scoring with IDF weighting for sparse inverted-index retrieval.
AI.result: Achieves Recall@200 ≥ 0.993 across seven benchmarks, enabling two-stage pipeline with only 200 candidates per query while maintaining near-dense accuracy (within 0.2% average).
AI.conclusion: BM25-V provides an efficient, interpretable first-stage retriever with zero-shot transfer capability and attributable retrieval decisions via visual-word IDF contributions.

title: Proof-of-Guardrail in AI Agents and What (Not) to Trust from It
summary: As AI agents become widely deployed as online services, users often rely on an agent developer's claim about how safety is enforced, which introduces a threat where safety measures are falsely advertised. To address the threat, we propose proof-of-guardrail, a system that enables developers to provide cryptographic proof that a response is generated after a specific open-source guardrail. To generate proof, the developer runs the agent and guardrail inside a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE), which produces a TEE-signed attestation of guardrail code execution verifiable by any user offline. We implement proof-of-guardrail for OpenClaw agents and evaluate latency overhead and deployment cost. Proof-of-guardrail ensures integrity of guardrail execution while keeping the developer's agent private, but we also highlight a risk of deception about safety, for example, when malicious developers actively jailbreak the guardrail. Code and demo video: https://github.com/SaharaLabsAI/Verifiable-ClawGuard

AI.tldr: Proof-of-guardrail enables cryptographic verification that AI agent responses were generated after specific open-source guardrail execution, using TEE attestation to address false safety claims.
AI.motivation: Addresses the threat of falsely advertised safety measures in AI agents where users must trust developer claims about safety enforcement without verification.
AI.method: Developers run agents and guardrails inside Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) that produce TEE-signed attestations of guardrail code execution, verifiable offline by users.
AI.result: Implemented for OpenClaw agents with evaluation of latency overhead and deployment cost; ensures guardrail execution integrity while keeping developer's agent private.
AI.conclusion: Proof-of-guardrail provides verifiable safety enforcement but highlights risks of deception when malicious developers actively jailbreak guardrails.

title: StreamWise: Serving Multi-Modal Generation in Real-Time at Scale
summary:
Advances in multi-modal generative models are enabling new applications, from storytelling to automated media synthesis. Most current workloads generate simple outputs (e.g., image generation from a prompt) in batch mode, often requiring several seconds even for basic results. Serving real-time multi-modal workflows at scale is costly and complex, requiring efficient coordination of diverse models (each with unique resource needs) across language, audio, image, and video, all under strict latency and resource constraints.
  We tackle these challenges through the lens of real-time podcast video generation, integrating LLMs, text-to-speech, and video-audio generation. To meet tight SLOs, we design an adaptive, modular serving system, StreamWise, that dynamically manages quality (e.g., resolution, sharpness), model/content parallelism, and resource-aware scheduling. We leverage heterogeneous hardware to maximize responsiveness and efficiency. For example, the system can lower video resolution and allocate more resources to early scenes.
  We quantify the trade-offs between latency, cost, and quality. The cheapest setup generates a 10-minute podcast video on A100 GPUs in 1.4 hours (8.4x slower than the real-time) for less than \$25. StreamWise enables high-quality real-time streaming with a sub-second startup delay under $45.

AI.tldr: StreamWise: An adaptive serving system for real-time multi-modal generative workflows, specifically for podcast video generation, that dynamically manages quality, parallelism, and resource scheduling to meet strict latency constraints.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal generative models are too slow for real-time applications, requiring several seconds even for basic outputs. Serving real-time multi-modal workflows at scale is costly and complex due to diverse model requirements and strict latency constraints.
AI.method: Design an adaptive, modular serving system (StreamWise) that dynamically manages quality parameters (resolution, sharpness), model/content parallelism, and resource-aware scheduling. Leverages heterogeneous hardware and can adjust resource allocation (e.g., lower video resolution for early scenes to meet startup delay).
AI.result: Quantified trade-offs between latency, cost, and quality. Cheapest setup generates 10-minute podcast video on A100 GPUs in 1.4 hours (8.4x slower than real-time) for <$25. StreamWise enables high-quality real-time streaming with sub-second startup delay under $45.
AI.conclusion: StreamWise successfully addresses the challenges of real-time multi-modal generative workflows through adaptive quality management, resource-aware scheduling, and heterogeneous hardware utilization, enabling cost-effective real-time podcast video generation with strict latency SLOs.

title: Ambiguity Collapse by LLMs: A Taxonomy of Epistemic Risks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to make sense of ambiguous, open-textured, value-laden terms. Platforms routinely rely on LLMs for content moderation, asking them to label text based on disputed concepts like "hate speech" or "incitement"; hiring managers may use LLMs to rank who counts as "qualified"; and AI labs increasingly train models to self-regulate under constitutional-style ambiguous principles such as "biased" or "legitimate". This paper introduces ambiguity collapse: a phenomenon that occurs when an LLM encounters a term that genuinely admits multiple legitimate interpretations, yet produces a singular resolution, in ways that bypass the human practices through which meaning is ordinarily negotiated, contested, and justified. Drawing on interdisciplinary accounts of ambiguity as a productive epistemic resource, we develop a taxonomy of the epistemic risks posed by ambiguity collapse at three levels: process (foreclosing opportunities to deliberate, develop cognitive skills, and shape contested terms), output (distorting the concepts and reasons agents act upon), and ecosystem (reshaping shared vocabularies, interpretive norms, and how concepts evolve over time). We illustrate these risks through three case studies, and conclude by sketching multi-layer mitigation principles spanning training, institutional deployment design, interface affordances, and the management of underspecified prompts, with the goal of designing systems that surface, preserve, and responsibly govern ambiguity.

AI.tldr: LLMs collapse genuine ambiguity in contested terms like "hate speech" or "qualified," producing singular interpretations that bypass human meaning negotiation, posing epistemic risks at process, output, and ecosystem levels.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to interpret ambiguous, value-laden terms in content moderation, hiring, and AI self-regulation, but they collapse genuine ambiguity by producing singular resolutions that bypass human meaning negotiation practices.
AI.method: Introduces the concept of "ambiguity collapse," develops a taxonomy of epistemic risks at three levels (process, output, ecosystem), and illustrates risks through three case studies.
AI.result: Identifies that LLMs collapse legitimate ambiguity in contested terms, foreclosing deliberation opportunities, distorting concepts, and reshaping shared vocabularies and interpretive norms.
AI.conclusion: Proposes multi-layer mitigation principles spanning training, institutional deployment design, interface affordances, and prompt management to preserve and responsibly govern ambiguity in LLM systems.

title: Margin and Consistency Supervision for Calibrated and Robust Vision Models
summary: Deep vision classifiers often achieve high accuracy while remaining poorly calibrated and fragile under small distribution shifts. We present Margin and Consistency Supervision (MaCS), a simple, architecture-agnostic regularization framework that jointly enforces logit-space separation and local prediction stability. MaCS augments cross-entropy with (i) a hinge-squared margin penalty that enforces a target logit gap between the correct class and the strongest competitor, and (ii) a consistency regularizer that minimizes the KL divergence between predictions on clean inputs and mildly perturbed views. We provide a unifying theoretical analysis showing that increasing classification margin while reducing local sensitivity formalized via a Lipschitz-type stability proxy yields improved generalization guarantees and a provable robustness radius bound scaling with the margin-to-sensitivity ratio. Across several image classification benchmarks and several backbones spanning CNNs and Vision Transformers, MaCS consistently improves calibration (lower ECE and NLL) and robustness to common corruptions while preserving or improving top-1 accuracy. Our approach requires no additional data, no architectural changes, and negligible inference overhead, making it an effective drop-in replacement for standard training objectives.

AI.tldr: MaCS is a regularization framework that improves classifier calibration and robustness by enforcing logit margins and prediction consistency, requiring no architectural changes or extra data.
AI.motivation: Deep vision classifiers often achieve high accuracy but suffer from poor calibration and fragility under small distribution shifts, necessitating methods that improve both calibration and robustness without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: MaCS augments cross-entropy with two components: (1) a hinge-squared margin penalty enforcing target logit gap between correct class and strongest competitor, and (2) a consistency regularizer minimizing KL divergence between predictions on clean inputs and mildly perturbed views.
AI.result: Across multiple image classification benchmarks and backbones (CNNs and Vision Transformers), MaCS consistently improves calibration (lower ECE and NLL) and robustness to common corruptions while preserving or improving top-1 accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MaCS provides an effective drop-in replacement for standard training objectives that improves both calibration and robustness with no additional data, architectural changes, or inference overhead, supported by theoretical guarantees on generalization and robustness radius.

title: Lexara: A User-Centered Toolkit for Evaluating Large Language Models for Conversational Visual Analytics
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming Conversational Visual Analytics (CVA) by enabling data analysis through natural language. However, evaluating LLMs for CVA remains a challenge: requiring programming expertise, overlooking real-world complexity, and lacking interpretable metrics for multi-format (visualizations and text) outputs. Through interviews with 22 CVA developers and 16 end-users, we identified use cases, evaluation criteria and workflows. We present Lexara, a user-centered evaluation toolkit for CVA that operationalizes these insights into: (i) test cases spanning real-world scenarios; (ii) interpretable metrics covering visualization quality (data fidelity, semantic alignment, functional correctness, design clarity) and language quality (factual grounding, analytical reasoning, conversational coherence) using rule-based and LLM-as-a-Judge methods; and (iii) an interactive toolkit enabling experimental setup and multi-format and multi-level exploration of results without programming expertise. We conducted a two-week diary study with six CVA developers, drawn from our initial cohort of 22. Their feedback demonstrated Lexara's effectiveness for guiding appropriate model and prompt selection.

AI.tldr: Lexara is a user-centered evaluation toolkit for Conversational Visual Analytics (CVA) that addresses LLM evaluation challenges by providing real-world test cases, interpretable metrics for visualization and language quality, and an interactive interface for developers without programming expertise.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for LLMs in Conversational Visual Analytics require programming expertise, overlook real-world complexity, and lack interpretable metrics for multi-format outputs (visualizations and text).
AI.method: Conducted interviews with 22 CVA developers and 16 end-users to identify use cases, evaluation criteria, and workflows. Developed Lexara toolkit with: (i) real-world test cases, (ii) interpretable metrics covering visualization quality (data fidelity, semantic alignment, functional correctness, design clarity) and language quality (factual grounding, analytical reasoning, conversational coherence) using rule-based and LLM-as-a-Judge methods, and (iii) interactive toolkit for experimental setup and result exploration. Validated through two-week diary study with six CVA developers.
AI.result: Lexara effectively guided appropriate model and prompt selection for CVA developers, as demonstrated through user feedback from the diary study.
AI.conclusion: Lexara provides a practical, user-centered solution for evaluating LLMs in Conversational Visual Analytics, addressing key challenges in evaluation methodology and making LLM assessment accessible to developers without extensive programming expertise.

title: Evaluating LLM Alignment With Human Trust Models
comment: This paper will appear in the post-proceedings of ICAART 2026
summary: Trust plays a pivotal role in enabling effective cooperation, reducing uncertainty, and guiding decision-making in both human interactions and multi-agent systems.
