Although it is significant, there is limited understanding of how large language models (LLMs) internally conceptualize and reason about trust. This work presents a white-box analysis of trust representation in EleutherAI/gpt-j-6B, using contrastive prompting to generate embedding vectors within the activation space of the LLM for diadic trust and related interpersonal relationship attributes. We first identified trust-related concepts from five established human trust models. We then determined a threshold for significant conceptual alignment by computing pairwise cosine similarities across 60 general emotional concepts. Then we measured the cosine similarities between the LLM's internal representation of trust and the derived trust-related concepts. Our results show that the internal trust representation of EleutherAI/gpt-j-6B aligns most closely with the Castelfranchi socio-cognitive model, followed by the Marsh Model. These findings indicate that LLMs encode socio-cognitive constructs in their activation space in ways that support meaningful comparative analyses, inform theories of social cognition, and support the design of human-AI collaborative systems.

AI.tldr: White-box analysis reveals GPT-J-6B's internal trust representation aligns most with Castelfranchi socio-cognitive model, showing LLMs encode socio-cognitive constructs in activation space.
AI.motivation: Trust is crucial for cooperation and decision-making in multi-agent systems, but there's limited understanding of how LLMs internally conceptualize and reason about trust. The paper aims to analyze trust representation in LLMs using white-box methods.
AI.method: Used contrastive prompting to generate embedding vectors in GPT-J-6B's activation space for dyadic trust and related interpersonal attributes. Identified trust-related concepts from five established human trust models, established threshold via pairwise cosine similarities across 60 emotional concepts, then measured similarities between LLM's trust representation and derived concepts.
AI.result: GPT-J-6B's internal trust representation aligns most closely with Castelfranchi socio-cognitive model, followed by Marsh Model. LLMs encode socio-cognitive constructs in activation space in ways that support comparative analysis.
AI.conclusion: LLMs encode socio-cognitive constructs in their activation space, supporting meaningful comparative analyses, informing social cognition theories, and aiding human-AI collaborative system design.

title: Remote Sensing Image Classification Using Deep Ensemble Learning
summary: Remote sensing imagery plays a crucial role in many applications and requires accurate computerized classification techniques. Reliable classification is essential for transforming raw imagery into structured and usable information. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are mostly used for image classification, they excel at local feature extraction, but struggle to capture global contextual information. Vision Transformers (ViTs) address this limitation through self attention mechanisms that model long-range dependencies. Integrating CNNs and ViTs, therefore, leads to better performance than standalone architectures. However, the use of additional CNN and ViT components does not lead to further performance improvement and instead introduces a bottleneck caused by redundant feature representations. In this research, we propose a fusion model that combines the strengths of CNNs and ViTs for remote sensing image classification. To overcome the performance bottleneck, the proposed approach trains four independent fusion models that integrate CNN and ViT backbones and combine their outputs at the final prediction stage through ensembling. The proposed method achieves accuracy rates of 98.10 percent, 94.46 percent, and 95.45 percent on the UC Merced, RSSCN7, and MSRSI datasets, respectively. These results outperform competing architectures and highlight the effectiveness of the proposed solution, particularly due to its efficient use of computational resources during training.

AI.tldr: Proposes an ensemble fusion model combining CNN and Vision Transformer backbones for remote sensing image classification, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on three benchmark datasets while addressing redundancy bottlenecks in hybrid architectures.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing requires accurate classification techniques. CNNs excel at local features but struggle with global context, while Vision Transformers capture long-range dependencies through self-attention. Hybrid CNN-ViT models offer better performance than standalone architectures, but current fusion approaches suffer from redundancy bottlenecks that limit further improvement.
AI.method: Proposes a fusion model combining CNN and ViT strengths. To overcome redundancy bottlenecks, trains four independent fusion models integrating CNN and ViT backbones, then combines their outputs at the final prediction stage through ensemble learning. This approach avoids redundant feature representations while leveraging complementary strengths of both architectures.
AI.result: Achieves accuracy rates of 98.10% on UC Merced dataset, 94.46% on RSSCN7 dataset, and 95.45% on MSRSI dataset. These results outperform competing architectures and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance. The method also shows efficient computational resource utilization during training.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ensemble fusion approach effectively combines CNN and Vision Transformer strengths for remote sensing image classification, overcoming redundancy bottlenecks in hybrid architectures. The method achieves superior accuracy on benchmark datasets while maintaining computational efficiency, demonstrating the effectiveness of ensemble-based fusion over direct architectural integration.

title: Computational Pathology in the Era of Emerging Foundation and Agentic AI -- International Expert Perspectives on Clinical Integration and Translational Readiness
summary: Recent breakthroughs in artificial intelligence through foundation models and agents have accelerated the evolution of computational pathology. Demonstrated performance gains reported across academia in benchmarking datasets in predictive tasks such as diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment response have ignited substantial enthusiasm for clinical application. Despite this development momentum, real world adoption has lagged, as implementation faces economic, technical, and administrative challenges. Beyond existing discussions of technical architectures and comparative performance, this review considers how these emerging AI systems can be responsibly integrated into medical practice by connecting deployable clinical relevance with downstream analytical capabilities and their technical maturity, operational readiness, and economic and regulatory context. Drawing on perspectives from an international group, we provide a practical assessment of current capabilities and barriers to adoption in patient care settings.

AI.tldr: Review examines barriers to clinical adoption of AI in computational pathology despite technical advances, focusing on practical implementation challenges beyond performance metrics.
AI.motivation: Despite AI breakthroughs in computational pathology showing strong performance in predictive tasks, real-world clinical adoption has lagged due to economic, technical, and administrative barriers beyond just technical capabilities.
AI.method: Review paper drawing on international perspectives to assess AI integration in pathology, connecting clinical relevance with technical maturity, operational readiness, and economic/regulatory context.
AI.result: Provides practical assessment of current AI capabilities and identifies barriers to adoption in patient care settings, moving beyond technical performance to implementation challenges.
AI.conclusion: Successful clinical integration of AI in pathology requires addressing implementation challenges in addition to technical performance, considering operational, economic, and regulatory factors alongside analytical capabilities.

title: Reconstruct! Don't Encode: Self-Supervised Representation Reconstruction Loss for High-Intelligibility and Low-Latency Streaming Neural Audio Codec
summary: Neural audio codecs optimized for mel-spectrogram reconstruction often fail to preserve intelligibility. While semantic encoder distillation improves encoded representations, it does not guarantee content preservation in reconstructed speech. In this work, we demonstrate that self-supervised representation reconstruction (SSRR) loss fundamentally improves codec training and performance. First, SSRR significantly accelerates convergence, enabling competitive results using only a single GPU. Second, it enhances intelligibility by reconstructing distilled self-supervised representations from codec outputs. Third, SSRR enables high intelligibility without additional lookahead in streaming Transformer-based codecs, allowing a zero-lookahead architecture for real-time deployment. As a result, our JHCodec achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining minimal latency and reduced training cost. We open-source the full implementation, training pipeline, and demo on Github https://github.com/jhcodec843/jhcodec.

AI.tldr: JHCodec uses self-supervised representation reconstruction (SSRR) loss to improve neural audio codec training, achieving better intelligibility, faster convergence, and zero-lookahead streaming capability with minimal latency.
AI.motivation: Neural audio codecs optimized for mel-spectrogram reconstruction often fail to preserve speech intelligibility, and while semantic encoder distillation helps, it doesn't guarantee content preservation in reconstructed speech.
AI.method: Proposes self-supervised representation reconstruction (SSRR) loss that reconstructs distilled self-supervised representations from codec outputs, enabling faster convergence and improved intelligibility without additional lookahead in streaming Transformer-based codecs.
AI.result: JHCodec achieves state-of-the-art performance with minimal latency, reduced training cost (competitive results using only a single GPU), and enables zero-lookahead architecture for real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: SSRR loss fundamentally improves codec training by accelerating convergence, enhancing intelligibility, and enabling high-performance streaming codecs with minimal latency, making JHCodec a practical solution for real-time applications.

title: Lost in Stories: Consistency Bugs in Long Story Generation by LLMs
summary: What happens when a storyteller forgets its own story? Large Language Models (LLMs) can now generate narratives spanning tens of thousands of words, but they often fail to maintain consistency throughout. When generating long-form narratives, these models can contradict their own established facts, character traits, and world rules. Existing story generation benchmarks focus mainly on plot quality and fluency, leaving consistency errors largely unexplored. To address this gap, we present ConStory-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate narrative consistency in long-form story generation. It contains 2,000 prompts across four task scenarios and defines a taxonomy of five error categories with 19 fine-grained subtypes. We also develop ConStory-Checker, an automated pipeline that detects contradictions and grounds each judgment in explicit textual evidence. Evaluating a range of LLMs through five research questions, we find that consistency errors show clear tendencies: they are most common in factual and temporal dimensions, tend to appear around the middle of narratives, occur in text segments with higher token-level entropy, and certain error types tend to co-occur. These findings can inform future efforts to improve consistency in long-form narrative generation. Our project page is available at https://picrew.github.io/constory-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ConStory-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating narrative consistency in long-form story generation, featuring 2,000 prompts across four scenarios with a taxonomy of 19 error subtypes, plus an automated detection pipeline called ConStory-Checker.
AI.motivation: LLMs generate long-form narratives but often fail to maintain consistency, contradicting established facts, character traits, and world rules. Existing benchmarks focus on plot quality and fluency, leaving consistency errors largely unexplored.
AI.method: Developed ConStory-Bench with 2,000 prompts across four task scenarios, defined a taxonomy of five error categories with 19 fine-grained subtypes, and created ConStory-Checker - an automated pipeline that detects contradictions and grounds judgments in explicit textual evidence.
AI.result: Evaluation of various LLMs revealed that consistency errors are most common in factual and temporal dimensions, tend to appear around the middle of narratives, occur in text segments with higher token-level entropy, and certain error types tend to co-occur.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into consistency error patterns in long-form narrative generation and can inform future efforts to improve consistency. The benchmark and detection tools are publicly available.

title: Reference-guided Policy Optimization for Molecular Optimization via LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) benefit substantially from supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in reasoning tasks. However, these recipes perform poorly in instruction-based molecular optimization, where each data point typically provides only a single optimized reference molecule and no step-by-step optimization trajectory. We reveal that answer-only SFT on the reference molecules collapses reasoning, and RLVR provides sparse feedback under similarity constraints due to the model's lack of effective exploration, which slows learning and limits optimization. To encourage the exploration of new molecules while balancing the exploitation of the reference molecules, we introduce Reference-guided Policy Optimization (RePO), an optimization approach that learns from reference molecules without requiring trajectory data. At each update, RePO samples candidate molecules with their intermediate reasoning trajectories from the model and trains the model using verifiable rewards that measure property satisfaction under similarity constraints in an RL manner. Meanwhile, it applies reference guidance by keeping the policy's intermediate reasoning trajectory as context and training only the answer in a supervised manner. Together, the RL term promotes exploration, while the guidance term mitigates reward sparsity and stabilizes training by grounding outputs to references when many valid molecular edits exist. Across molecular optimization benchmarks, RePO consistently outperforms SFT and RLVR baselines (e.g., GRPO), achieving improvements on the optimization metric (Success Rate $\times$ Similarity), improving balance across competing objectives, and generalizing better to unseen instruction styles. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/RePO.

AI.tldr: RePO is a novel optimization approach for instruction-based molecular optimization that combines reinforcement learning with reference guidance to overcome limitations of SFT and RLVR when only single optimized reference molecules are available.
AI.motivation: Current LLM fine-tuning methods (SFT and RLVR) perform poorly in molecular optimization tasks where only single optimized reference molecules are available without step-by-step trajectories. SFT collapses reasoning, while RLVR suffers from sparse feedback and ineffective exploration under similarity constraints.
AI.method: Reference-guided Policy Optimization (RePO) combines RL with reference guidance: samples candidate molecules with reasoning trajectories, uses verifiable rewards for property satisfaction under similarity constraints (RL term), and applies reference guidance by keeping policy's intermediate reasoning as context while training only the answer in supervised manner.
AI.result: RePO consistently outperforms SFT and RLVR baselines (including GRPO) across molecular optimization benchmarks, achieving improvements on Success Rate × Similarity metric, better balance across competing objectives, and superior generalization to unseen instruction styles.
AI.conclusion: RePO effectively addresses the exploration-exploitation trade-off in molecular optimization by combining RL exploration with reference guidance, enabling effective learning from single reference molecules without requiring trajectory data.

title: LUMINA: LLM-Guided GPU Architecture Exploration via Bottleneck Analysis
summary:
GPU design space exploration (DSE) for modern AI workloads, such as Large-Language Model (LLM) inference, is challenging because of GPUs' vast, multi-modal design spaces, high simulation costs, and complex design optimization objectives (e.g. performance, power and area trade-offs). Existing automated DSE methods are often prohibitively expensive, either requiring an excessive number of exploration samples or depending on intricate, manually crafted analyses of interdependent critical paths guided by human heuristics.
  We present LUMINA, an LLM-driven GPU architecture exploration framework that leverage AI to enhance the DSE efficiency and efficacy for GPUs. LUMINA extracts architectural knowledge from simulator code and performs sensitivity studies to automatically compose DSE rules,which are auto-corrected during exploration. A core component of LUMINA is a DSE Benchmark that comprehensively evaluates and enhances LLMs' capabilities across three fundamental skills required for architecture optimization, which provides a principled and reproducible basis for model selection and ensuring consistent architectural reasoning.
  In the design space with 4.7 million possible samples, LUMINA identifies 6 designs of better performance and area than an A100 GPU efficiently, using only 20 steps via LLM-assisted bottleneck analysis. In comparison, LUMINA achieves 17.5x higher than design space exploration efficiency, and 32.9% better designs (i.e. Pareto Hypervolume) than Machine-Learning baselines, showcasing its ability to deliver high-quality design guidance with minimal search cost.

AI.tldr: LUMINA is an LLM-driven GPU architecture exploration framework that uses AI to efficiently navigate vast GPU design spaces for AI workloads like LLM inference, achieving 17.5x higher exploration efficiency than baselines.
AI.motivation: GPU design space exploration for modern AI workloads is challenging due to vast multi-modal design spaces, high simulation costs, and complex optimization objectives. Existing automated methods are prohibitively expensive, requiring excessive samples or manual heuristic analysis.
AI.method: LUMINA extracts architectural knowledge from simulator code, performs sensitivity studies to automatically compose DSE rules (auto-corrected during exploration), and includes a DSE Benchmark to evaluate LLMs' architecture optimization skills for principled model selection.
AI.result: In a design space with 4.7 million possible samples, LUMINA identified 6 designs with better performance and area than an A100 GPU using only 20 steps. It achieved 17.5x higher exploration efficiency and 32.9% better designs (Pareto Hypervolume) than ML baselines.
AI.conclusion: LUMINA demonstrates that LLM-driven frameworks can deliver high-quality GPU design guidance with minimal search cost, effectively addressing the challenges of GPU architecture exploration for AI workloads through automated knowledge extraction and intelligent bottleneck analysis.

title: CORE-Seg: Reasoning-Driven Segmentation for Complex Lesions via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Under Review with Computational Visual Media
summary: Medical image segmentation is undergoing a paradigm shift from conventional visual pattern matching to cognitive reasoning analysis. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in integrating linguistic and visual knowledge, significant gaps remain: existing general MLLMs possess broad common sense but lack the specialized visual reasoning required for complex lesions, whereas traditional segmentation models excel at pixel-level segmentation but lack logical interpretability. In this paper, we introduce ComLesion-14K, the first diverse Chain-of-Thought (CoT) benchmark for reasoning-driven complex lesion segmentation. To accomplish this task, we propose CORE-Seg, an end-to-end framework integrating reasoning with segmentation through a Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter. We design a progressive training strategy from SFT to GRPO, equipped with an adaptive dual-granularity reward mechanism to mitigate reward sparsity. Our Method achieves state-of-the-art results with a mean Dice of 37.06\% (14.89\% higher than the second-best baseline), while reducing the failure rate to 18.42\%. Project Page: https://xyxl024.github.io/CORE-Seg.github.io/

AI.tldr: CORE-Seg introduces a reasoning-driven framework for complex lesion segmentation, combining Chain-of-Thought reasoning with segmentation through a Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the new ComLesion-14K benchmark.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation needs to evolve from visual pattern matching to cognitive reasoning analysis. Current MLLMs lack specialized visual reasoning for complex lesions, while traditional segmentation models lack logical interpretability, creating a gap that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: Proposes CORE-Seg, an end-to-end framework integrating reasoning with segmentation via Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter. Uses progressive training from SFT to GRPO with adaptive dual-granularity reward mechanism to mitigate reward sparsity. Built on the new ComLesion-14K benchmark with diverse Chain-of-Thought annotations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results with mean Dice of 37.06% (14.89% higher than second-best baseline) and reduces failure rate to 18.42% on the ComLesion-14K benchmark.
AI.conclusion: CORE-Seg successfully bridges the gap between reasoning and segmentation for complex lesions, demonstrating that integrating cognitive reasoning with segmentation significantly improves performance on challenging medical imaging tasks.

title: Stock Market Prediction Using Node Transformer Architecture Integrated with BERT Sentiment Analysis
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables, submitted to IEEE Access
summary: Stock market prediction presents considerable challenges for investors, financial institutions, and policymakers operating in complex market environments characterized by noise, non-stationarity, and behavioral dynamics. Traditional forecasting methods often fail to capture the intricate patterns and cross-sectional dependencies inherent in financial markets. This paper presents an integrated framework combining a node transformer architecture with BERT-based sentiment analysis for stock price forecasting. The proposed model represents the stock market as a graph structure where individual stocks form nodes and edges capture relationships including sectoral affiliations, correlated price movements, and supply chain connections. A fine-tuned BERT model extracts sentiment from social media posts and combines it with quantitative market features through attention-based fusion. The node transformer processes historical market data while capturing both temporal evolution and cross-sectional dependencies among stocks. Experiments on 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning January 1982 to March 2025 demonstrate that the integrated model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.80% for one-day-ahead predictions, compared to 1.20% for ARIMA and 1.00% for LSTM. Sentiment analysis reduces prediction error by 10% overall and 25% during earnings announcements, while graph-based modeling contributes an additional 15% improvement by capturing inter-stock dependencies. Directional accuracy reaches 65% for one-day forecasts. Statistical validation through paired t-tests confirms these improvements (p < 0.05 for all comparisons). The model maintains MAPE below 1.5% during high-volatility periods where baseline models exceed 2%.

AI.tldr: Integrated framework combining node transformer architecture with BERT-based sentiment analysis for stock price forecasting, achieving 0.80% MAPE for one-day-ahead predictions with 65% directional accuracy.
AI.motivation: Stock market prediction is challenging due to complex market environments with noise, non-stationarity, and behavioral dynamics. Traditional methods fail to capture intricate patterns and cross-sectional dependencies inherent in financial markets.
AI.method: Integrated framework representing stock market as graph structure with stocks as nodes and relationships as edges (sectoral affiliations, correlated price movements, supply chain connections). Combines fine-tuned BERT model for sentiment analysis from social media with quantitative market features through attention-based fusion. Node transformer processes historical market data capturing both temporal evolution and cross-sectional dependencies.
AI.result: Achieves 0.80% MAPE for one-day-ahead predictions vs. 1.20% for ARIMA and 1.00% for LSTM. Sentiment analysis reduces prediction error by 10% overall and 25% during earnings announcements. Graph-based modeling contributes additional 15% improvement. Directional accuracy reaches 65% for one-day forecasts. Statistical validation confirms improvements (p < 0.05). Maintains MAPE below 1.5% during high-volatility periods where baselines exceed 2%.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework combining node transformer architecture with BERT-based sentiment analysis effectively addresses stock market prediction challenges by capturing both temporal patterns and cross-sectional dependencies, significantly outperforming traditional methods.

title: BlackMirror: Black-Box Backdoor Detection for Text-to-Image Models via Instruction-Response Deviation
comment: This paper is accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: This paper investigates the challenging task of detecting backdoored text-to-image models under black-box settings and introduces a novel detection framework BlackMirror. Existing approaches typically rely on analyzing image-level similarity, under the assumption that backdoor-triggered generations exhibit strong consistency across samples. However, they struggle to generalize to recently emerging backdoor attacks, where backdoored generations can appear visually diverse. BlackMirror is motivated by an observation: across backdoor attacks, {only partial semantic patterns within the generated image are steadily manipulated, while the rest of the content remains diverse or benign. Accordingly, BlackMirror consists of two components: MirrorMatch, which aligns visual patterns with the corresponding instructions to detect semantic deviations; and MirrorVerify, which evaluates the stability of these deviations across varied prompts to distinguish true backdoor behavior from benign responses. BlackMirror is a general, training-free framework that can be deployed as a plug-and-play module in Model-as-a-Service (MaaS) applications. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that BlackMirror achieves accurate detection across a wide range of attacks. Code is available at https://github.com/Ferry-Li/BlackMirror.

AI.tldr: BlackMirror is a novel black-box detection framework for identifying backdoored text-to-image models by analyzing partial semantic pattern manipulation rather than full image similarity, using MirrorMatch to detect semantic deviations and MirrorVerify to evaluate deviation stability across prompts.
AI.motivation: Existing detection approaches rely on image-level similarity analysis, assuming backdoor-triggered generations exhibit strong consistency. However, these methods fail against emerging backdoor attacks where backdoored generations appear visually diverse, necessitating a new detection paradigm.
AI.method: BlackMirror consists of two components: MirrorMatch aligns visual patterns with corresponding instructions to detect semantic deviations, and MirrorVerify evaluates the stability of these deviations across varied prompts to distinguish true backdoor behavior from benign responses. It's a training-free, plug-and-play framework.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that BlackMirror achieves accurate detection across a wide range of attacks, outperforming existing methods that rely on image-level similarity analysis.
AI.conclusion: BlackMirror provides an effective black-box detection framework for backdoored text-to-image models by focusing on partial semantic pattern manipulation rather than full image similarity, offering a general, training-free solution deployable in Model-as-a-Service applications.

title: RAC: Rectified Flow Auto Coder
comment: 11 Figures, 4 Tables. Project Page at https://world-snapshot.github.io/RAC/
summary: In this paper, we propose a Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC) inspired by Rectified Flow to replace the traditional VAE: 1. It achieves multi-step decoding by applying the decoder to flow timesteps. Its decoding path is straight and correctable, enabling step-by-step refinement. 2. The model inherently supports bidirectional inference, where the decoder serves as the encoder through time reversal (hence Coder rather than encoder or decoder), reducing parameter count by nearly 41%. 3. This generative decoding method improves generation quality since the model can correct latent variables along the path, partially addressing the reconstruction--generation gap. Experiments show that RAC surpasses SOTA VAEs in both reconstruction and generation with approximately 70% lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: RAC replaces traditional VAE with a Rectified Flow Auto Coder that enables multi-step straight-path decoding with correction capabilities, bidirectional inference through time reversal, and improved generation quality with 70% lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of traditional VAEs including the reconstruction-generation gap, computational inefficiency, and lack of refinement capabilities during decoding.
AI.method: Proposes Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC) that applies decoder to flow timesteps for multi-step decoding with straight, correctable paths; uses time reversal for bidirectional inference where decoder serves as encoder; enables latent variable correction along decoding path.
AI.result: RAC surpasses state-of-the-art VAEs in both reconstruction and generation quality with approximately 70% lower computational cost and 41% parameter reduction.
AI.conclusion: RAC provides an efficient alternative to VAEs with improved generation quality through straight-path decoding with correction capabilities and bidirectional inference via time reversal.

title: Addressing the Ecological Fallacy in Larger LMs with Human Context
summary: Language model training and inference ignore a fundamental linguistic fact -- there is a dependence between multiple sequences of text written by the same person. Prior work has shown that addressing this form of \textit{ecological fallacy} can greatly improve the performance of multiple smaller (~124M) GPT-based models. In this work, we ask if addressing the ecological fallacy by modeling the author's language context with a specific LM task (called HuLM) can provide similar benefits for a larger-scale model, an 8B Llama model. To this end, we explore variants that process an author's language in the context of their other temporally ordered texts. We study the effect of pre-training with this author context using the HuLM objective, as well as using it during fine-tuning with author context (\textit{HuFT:Human-aware Fine-Tuning}). Empirical comparisons show that addressing the ecological fallacy during fine-tuning alone using QLoRA improves the performance of the larger 8B model over standard fine-tuning. Additionally, QLoRA-based continued HuLM pre-training results in a human-aware model generalizable for improved performance over eight downstream tasks with linear task classifier training alone. These results indicate the utility and importance of modeling language in the context of its original generators, the authors.

AI.tldr: Addressing ecological fallacy in language models by incorporating author context improves performance of 8B Llama models through HuLM pre-training and HuFT fine-tuning approaches.
AI.motivation: Current language models ignore the dependence between multiple text sequences written by the same author, which represents an ecological fallacy. Prior work showed addressing this improves smaller GPT models, but it's unknown if similar benefits apply to larger-scale models like 8B Llama.
AI.method: Developed HuLM (Human-aware Language Modeling) objective to model author's language context using their temporally ordered texts. Explored two approaches: 1) HuLM pre-training with author context, and 2) HuFT (Human-aware Fine-Tuning) using author context during fine-tuning. Used QLoRA for efficient fine-tuning of the 8B Llama model.
AI.result: HuFT with QLoRA improved performance over standard fine-tuning. Continued HuLM pre-training with QLoRA produced human-aware models that generalized well, showing improved performance across eight downstream tasks with only linear classifier training.
AI.conclusion: Modeling language in the context of its original authors (addressing ecological fallacy) provides significant utility and importance for larger-scale language models, with both pre-training and fine-time approaches yielding benefits.

title: Facial Expression Recognition Using Residual Masking Network
summary: Automatic facial expression recognition (FER) has gained much attention due to its applications in human-computer interaction. Among the approaches to improve FER tasks, this paper focuses on deep architecture with the attention mechanism. We propose a novel Masking idea to boost the performance of CNN in facial expression task. It uses a segmentation network to refine feature maps, enabling the network to focus on relevant information to make correct decisions. In experiments, we combine the ubiquitous Deep Residual Network and Unet-like architecture to produce a Residual Masking Network. The proposed method holds state-of-the-art (SOTA) accuracy on the well-known FER2013 and private VEMO datasets. The source code is available at https://github.com/phamquiluan/ResidualMaskingNetwork.

AI.tldr: A novel Residual Masking Network combining Deep Residual Network and Unet-like architecture with attention mechanism for facial expression recognition, achieving SOTA accuracy on FER2013 and VEMO datasets.
AI.motivation: To improve facial expression recognition (FER) performance by enhancing deep architectures with attention mechanisms, specifically addressing the need for networks to focus on relevant facial features while ignoring irrelevant information.
AI.method: Proposes a Masking idea using a segmentation network to refine feature maps, enabling focus on relevant information. Combines Deep Residual Network with Unet-like architecture to create Residual Masking Network, which uses attention mechanisms to mask irrelevant features.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on both the public FER2013 dataset and private VEMO dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The Residual Masking Network effectively improves FER performance by incorporating attention mechanisms through masking, providing a promising approach for human-computer interaction applications.

title: XAI for Coding Agent Failures: Transforming Raw Execution Traces into Actionable Insights
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based coding agents show promise in automating software development tasks, yet they frequently fail in ways that are difficult for developers to understand and debug. While general-purpose LLMs like GPT can provide ad-hoc explanations of failures, raw execution traces remain challenging to interpret even for experienced developers. We present a systematic explainable AI (XAI) approach that transforms raw agent execution traces into structured, human-interpretable explanations. Our method consists of three key components: (1) a domain-specific failure taxonomy derived from analyzing real agent failures, (2) an automatic annotation system that classifies failures using defined annotation schema, (3) a hybrid explanation generator that produces visual execution flows, natural language explanations, and actionable recommendations. Through a user study with 20 participants (10 technical, 10 non-technical), we demonstrate that our approach enables users to identify failure root causes 2.8 times faster and propose correct fixes with 73% higher accuracy compared to raw execution traces. Importantly, our structured approach outperforms ad-hoc state of the art models explanations by providing consistent, domain-specific insights with integrated visualizations. Our work establishes a framework for systematic agent failure analysis, addressing the critical need for interpretable AI systems in software development workflows

AI.tldr: A systematic XAI approach transforms raw LLM-based coding agent execution traces into structured, human-interpretable explanations using a failure taxonomy, automatic annotation, and hybrid explanation generation, improving debugging efficiency and fix accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLM-based coding agents frequently fail in ways that are difficult for developers to understand and debug, with raw execution traces being challenging to interpret even for experienced developers. There's a critical need for interpretable AI systems in software development workflows.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) domain-specific failure taxonomy derived from analyzing real agent failures, (2) automatic annotation system that classifies failures using defined annotation schema, (3) hybrid explanation generator that produces visual execution flows, natural language explanations, and actionable recommendations.
AI.result: User study with 20 participants (10 technical, 10 non-technical) showed users identify failure root causes 2.8 times faster and propose correct fixes with 73% higher accuracy compared to raw execution traces. The structured approach outperforms ad-hoc state-of-the-art model explanations.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a framework for systematic agent failure analysis, addressing the critical need for interpretable AI systems in software development workflows by providing consistent, domain-specific insights with integrated visualizations.

title: Energy-Driven Adaptive Visual Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Models
summary: Visual token reduction is critical for accelerating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet most existing approaches rely on a fixed budget shared across all inputs, overlooking the substantial variation in image information density. We propose E-AdaPrune, an energy-driven adaptive pruning framework that determines the token budget from the singular value spectrum of the visual features space. By preserving a certain proportion of spectral energy, our method allocates more tokens to information-dense scenes while aggressively compressing redundant ones, without introducing additional learnable parameters. We evaluate E-AdaPrune on nine benchmarks and three VLM backbones, LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, and LLaVA-NeXT-8B. Under matched average token budgets, E-AdaPrune consistently yields an average improvement of up to 0.6\%, including a significant +5.1\% relative boost on the MMVet reasoning task. Using randomized singular value decomposition, the additional latency is limited to 8ms per image.

AI.tldr: E-AdaPrune is an energy-driven adaptive pruning framework for Vision-Language Models that dynamically determines token budgets based on image information density using singular value spectrum analysis, achieving better performance with fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing visual token reduction approaches use fixed budgets across all inputs, ignoring the significant variation in image information density. This one-size-fits-all approach is inefficient as it doesn't adapt to the actual information content of each image.
AI.method: The method uses singular value decomposition of visual features to measure information density. By preserving a certain proportion of spectral energy, it dynamically allocates token budgets - more tokens for information-dense scenes and aggressive compression for redundant ones. No additional learnable parameters are introduced, and randomized SVD keeps latency low (8ms per image).
AI.result: Evaluated on 9 benchmarks with 3 VLM backbones (LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, LLaVA-NeXT-8B). Under matched average token budgets, achieved average improvement up to 0.6%, with significant +5.1% relative boost on MMVet reasoning task. Maintains performance while reducing computational cost.
AI.conclusion: E-AdaPrune provides an effective, parameter-free approach to adaptive token pruning that improves VLM efficiency by dynamically allocating tokens based on image information density, outperforming fixed-budget methods while adding minimal computational overhead.

title: Who We Are, Where We Are: Mental Health at the Intersection of Person, Situation, and Large Language Models
summary: Mental health is not a fixed trait but a dynamic process shaped by the interplay between individual dispositions and situational contexts. Building on interactionist and constructionist psychological theories, we develop interpretable models to predict well-being and identify adaptive and maladaptive self-states in longitudinal social media data. Our approach integrates person-level psychological traits (e.g., resilience, cognitive distortions, implicit motives) with language-inferred situational features derived from the Situational 8 DIAMONDS framework. We compare these theory-grounded features to embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model that captures temporal and individual-specific patterns. Results show that our principled, theory-driven features provide competitive performance while offering greater interpretability. Qualitative analyses further highlight the psychological coherence of features most predictive of well-being. These findings underscore the value of integrating computational modeling with psychological theory to assess dynamic mental states in contextually sensitive and human-understandable ways.

AI.tldr: The paper develops interpretable models for predicting well-being and identifying self-states using psychological traits and situational features from social media data, showing theory-driven features offer competitive performance with better interpretability than language model embeddings.
AI.motivation: Mental health is dynamic and shaped by person-situation interactions, requiring models that integrate psychological theory with computational methods to understand well-being in contextually sensitive and interpretable ways.
AI.method: Integrates person-level psychological traits (resilience, cognitive distortions, implicit motives) with language-inferred situational features from the Situational 8 DIAMONDS framework, comparing these to embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model on longitudinal social media data.
AI.result: Theory-grounded features provide competitive predictive performance for well-being while offering greater interpretability than language model embeddings; qualitative analyses show psychological coherence of predictive features.
AI.conclusion: Integrating computational modeling with psychological theory enables assessment of dynamic mental states in contextually sensitive and human-understandable ways, highlighting the value of theory-driven approaches for mental health research.

title: Domain-Adaptive Model Merging across Disconnected Modes
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 3 tables; Accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Learning across domains is challenging when data cannot be centralized due to privacy or heterogeneity, which limits the ability to train a single comprehensive model. Model merging provides an appealing alternative by consolidating knowledge from multiple specialized models into one, avoiding data sharing and reducing retraining cost. In this work, we present DMM, a data-free model merging framework designed to handle highly divergent models. DMM proceeds in three steps. First, domain-specific models are trained independently. Second, models with high similarity are merged using standard techniques to ensure stability. Third, we synthesize pseudo-data from normalization statistics and distill knowledge from divergent models into the merged model through a lightweight refinement guided by these samples. This approach preserves rare but critical knowledge while maintaining stability. Extensive experiments on unimodal and multimodal benchmarks show that DMM achieves state-of-the-art performance over existing merging methods.

AI.tldr: DMM is a data-free model merging framework that handles divergent models through similarity-based merging and pseudo-data distillation.
AI.motivation: Centralized learning is limited by privacy and heterogeneity constraints; model merging offers an alternative by consolidating knowledge without data sharing or costly retraining.
AI.method: Three-step approach: 1) Train domain-specific models independently, 2) Merge similar models using standard techniques for stability, 3) Synthesize pseudo-data from normalization statistics and distill knowledge from divergent models into merged model via lightweight refinement.
AI.result: DMM achieves state-of-the-art performance over existing merging methods on unimodal and multimodal benchmarks while preserving rare but critical knowledge and maintaining stability.
AI.conclusion: DMM provides an effective data-free framework for merging highly divergent models, addressing privacy and heterogeneity constraints while achieving superior performance through similarity-based merging and pseudo-data distillation.

title: Skeleton-to-Image Encoding: Enabling Skeleton Representation Learning via Vision-Pretrained Models
comment: Submitted to IEEE TPAMI, under review
summary: Recent advances in large-scale pretrained vision models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across a wide range of downstream tasks, including cross-modal and multi-modal scenarios. However, their direct application to 3D human skeleton data remains challenging due to fundamental differences in data format. Moreover, the scarcity of large-scale skeleton datasets and the need to incorporate skeleton data into multi-modal action recognition without introducing additional model branches present significant research opportunities. To address these challenges, we introduce Skeleton-to-Image Encoding (S2I), a novel representation that transforms skeleton sequences into image-like data by partitioning and arranging joints based on body-part semantics and resizing to standardized image dimensions. This encoding enables, for the first time, the use of powerful vision-pretrained models for self-supervised skeleton representation learning, effectively transferring rich visual-domain knowledge to skeleton analysis. While existing skeleton methods often design models tailored to specific, homogeneous skeleton formats, they overlook the structural heterogeneity that naturally arises from diverse data sources. In contrast, our S2I representation offers a unified image-like format that naturally accommodates heterogeneous skeleton data. Extensive experiments on NTU-60, NTU-120, and PKU-MMD demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of our method for self-supervised skeleton representation learning, including under challenging cross-format evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: Skeleton-to-Image Encoding (S2I) transforms 3D skeleton sequences into image-like representations to enable vision-pretrained models for self-supervised skeleton learning, addressing format heterogeneity and data scarcity.
AI.motivation: Direct application of vision-pretrained models to 3D skeleton data is challenging due to format differences, scarcity of large-scale skeleton datasets, and the need to incorporate skeleton data into multi-modal action recognition without additional model branches. Existing methods overlook structural heterogeneity from diverse data sources.
AI.method: Skeleton-to-Image Encoding (S2I) transforms skeleton sequences into image-like data by partitioning and arranging joints based on body-part semantics and resizing to standardized image dimensions, enabling use of vision-pretrained models for self-supervised skeleton representation learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on NTU-60, NTU-120, and PKU-MMD demonstrate effectiveness and generalizability for self-supervised skeleton representation learning, including challenging cross-format evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: S2I provides a unified image-like format that accommodates heterogeneous skeleton data and enables transfer of rich visual-domain knowledge to skeleton analysis through vision-pretrained models.

title: Imagine How To Change: Explicit Procedure Modeling for Change Captioning
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Code and models are available at https://github.com/BlueberryOreo/ProCap
summary: Change captioning generates descriptions that explicitly describe the differences between two visually similar images. Existing methods operate on static image pairs, thus ignoring the rich temporal dynamics of the change procedure, which is the key to understand not only what has changed but also how it occurs. We introduce ProCap, a novel framework that reformulates change modeling from static image comparison to dynamic procedure modeling. ProCap features a two-stage design: The first stage trains a procedure encoder to learn the change procedure from a sparse set of keyframes. These keyframes are obtained by automatically generating intermediate frames to make the implicit procedural dynamics explicit and then sampling them to mitigate redundancy. Then the encoder learns to capture the latent dynamics of these keyframes via a caption-conditioned, masked reconstruction task. The second stage integrates this trained encoder within an encoder-decoder model for captioning. Instead of relying on explicit frames from the previous stage -- a process incurring computational overhead and sensitivity to visual noise -- we introduce learnable procedure queries to prompt the encoder for inferring the latent procedure representation, which the decoder then translates into text. The entire model is then trained end-to-end with a captioning loss, ensuring the encoder's output is both temporally coherent and captioning-aligned. Experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ProCap. Code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/BlueberryOreo/ProCap

AI.tldr: ProCap reformulates change captioning from static image comparison to dynamic procedure modeling using a two-stage framework with procedure encoder and learnable procedure queries.
AI.motivation: Existing change captioning methods operate on static image pairs, ignoring the rich temporal dynamics of the change procedure, which is key to understanding not only what has changed but also how it occurs.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) Train a procedure encoder to learn change procedures from sparse keyframes via caption-conditioned masked reconstruction; 2) Integrate encoder within encoder-decoder model using learnable procedure queries to infer latent procedure representations for captioning.
AI.result: Experiments on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ProCap in generating temporally coherent and captioning-aligned change descriptions.
AI.conclusion: ProCap successfully reformulates change modeling from static image comparison to dynamic procedure modeling, capturing both what changed and how it occurred through latent procedure representations.

title: Technical Report: Automated Optical Inspection of Surgical Instruments
summary: In the dynamic landscape of modern healthcare, maintaining the highest standards in surgical instruments is critical for clinical success. This report explores the diverse realm of surgical instruments and their associated manufacturing defects, emphasizing their pivotal role in ensuring the safety of surgical procedures. With potentially fatal consequences arising from even minor defects, precision in manufacturing is paramount.The report addresses the identification and rectification of critical defects such as cracks, rust, and structural irregularities. Such scrutiny prevents substantial financial losses for manufacturers and, more crucially, safeguards patient lives. The collaboration with industry leaders Daddy D Pro and Dr. Frigz International, renowned trailblazers in the Sialkot surgical cluster, provides invaluable insights into the analysis of defects in Pakistani-made instruments. This partnership signifies a commitment to advancing automated defect detection methodologies, specifically through the integration of deep learning architectures including YOLOv8, ResNet-152, and EfficientNet-b4, thereby elevating quality standards in the manufacturing process. The scope of this report is to identify various surgical instruments manufactured in Pakistan and analyze their associated defects using a newly developed dataset of 4,414 high-resolution images. By focusing on quality assurance through Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) tools, this document serves as a resource for manufacturers, healthcare professionals, and regulatory bodies. The insights gained contribute to the enhancement of instrument standards, ensuring a more reliable healthcare environment through industry expertise and cutting-edge technology.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an automated defect detection system for surgical instruments using deep learning (YOLOv8, ResNet-152, EfficientNet-b4) on a dataset of 4,414 high-resolution images to identify critical defects like cracks, rust, and structural irregularities in Pakistani-made instruments.
AI.motivation: Maintaining high standards in surgical instruments is critical for patient safety, as even minor defects can have fatal consequences. The paper addresses the need for precision manufacturing and automated defect detection to prevent financial losses for manufacturers and, more importantly, safeguard patient lives in healthcare settings.
AI.method: The research involves collaboration with industry leaders in Pakistan's Sialkot surgical cluster. It employs deep learning architectures including YOLOv8, ResNet-152, and EfficientNet-b4 for automated defect detection. The methodology uses a newly developed dataset of 4,414 high-resolution images of surgical instruments and focuses on Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) tools for quality assurance.
AI.result: The paper develops an automated defect detection system capable of identifying critical defects such as cracks, rust, and structural irregularities in surgical instruments. The collaboration with Pakistani manufacturers provides valuable insights into defect analysis specific to instruments manufactured in Pakistan.
AI.conclusion: The integration of deep learning architectures for automated defect detection elevates quality standards in surgical instrument manufacturing. The research contributes to enhancing instrument standards through industry expertise and cutting-edge technology, ensuring a more reliable healthcare environment for manufacturers, healthcare professionals, and regulatory bodies.

title: TADPO: Reinforcement Learning Goes Off-road
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at ICRA 2026
summary: Off-road autonomous driving poses significant challenges such as navigating unmapped, variable terrain with uncertain and diverse dynamics. Addressing these challenges requires effective long-horizon planning and adaptable control. Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a promising solution by learning control policies directly from interaction. However, because off-road driving is a long-horizon task with low-signal rewards, standard RL methods are challenging to apply in this setting. We introduce TADPO, a novel policy gradient formulation that extends Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), leveraging off-policy trajectories for teacher guidance and on-policy trajectories for student exploration. Building on this, we develop a vision-based, end-to-end RL system for high-speed off-road driving, capable of navigating extreme slopes and obstacle-rich terrain. We demonstrate our performance in simulation and, importantly, zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a full-scale off-road vehicle. To our knowledge, this work represents the first deployment of RL-based policies on a full-scale off-road platform.

AI.tldr: TADPO is a novel RL policy gradient method that extends PPO for off-road autonomous driving, using off-policy trajectories for teacher guidance and on-policy trajectories for student exploration, enabling vision-based end-to-end control with successful sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Off-road autonomous driving presents unique challenges including unmapped variable terrain, uncertain dynamics, and the need for long-horizon planning. Standard RL methods struggle due to long-horizon tasks with low-signal rewards, necessitating a specialized approach for this domain.
AI.method: TADPO extends Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with a novel policy gradient formulation that leverages off-policy trajectories for teacher guidance and on-policy trajectories for student exploration. The system is vision-based and end-to-end, designed for high-speed off-road navigation.
AI.result: The method demonstrates successful performance in simulation and achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a full-scale off-road vehicle, navigating extreme slopes and obstacle-rich terrain. This represents the first deployment of RL-based policies on a full-scale off-road platform.
AI.conclusion: TADPO effectively addresses the challenges of off-road autonomous driving by combining teacher guidance from off-policy data with student exploration, enabling practical RL deployment in real-world off-road environments through successful sim-to-real transfer.

title: MM-ISTS: Cooperating Irregularly Sampled Time Series Forecasting with Multimodal Vision-Text LLMs
summary: Irregularly sampled time series (ISTS) are widespread in real-world scenarios, exhibiting asynchronous observations on uneven time intervals across variables. Existing ISTS forecasting methods often solely utilize historical observations to predict future ones while falling short in learning contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal patterns. To address these problems, we achieve MM-ISTS, a multimodal framework augmented by vision-text large language models, that bridges temporal, visual, and textual modalities, facilitating ISTS forecasting. MM-ISTS encompasses a novel two-stage encoding mechanism. In particular, a cross-modal vision-text encoding module is proposed to automatically generate informative visual images and textual data, enabling the capture of intricate temporal patterns and comprehensive contextual understanding, in collaboration with multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). In parallel, ISTS encoding extracts complementary yet enriched temporal features from historical ISTS observations, including multi-view embedding fusion and a temporal-variable encoder. Further, we propose an adaptive query-based feature extractor to compress the learned tokens of MLLMs, filtering out small-scale useful knowledge, which in turn reduces computational costs. In addition, a multimodal alignment module with modality-aware gating is designed to alleviate the modality gap across ISTS, images, and text. Extensive experiments on real data offer insight into the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

AI.tldr: MM-ISTS: A multimodal framework using vision-text LLMs for irregularly sampled time series forecasting, integrating temporal, visual, and textual modalities through cross-modal encoding and adaptive feature extraction.
AI.motivation: Existing irregularly sampled time series forecasting methods rely solely on historical observations, lacking contextual semantics and fine-grained temporal pattern learning. Real-world ISTS have asynchronous observations on uneven intervals across variables.
AI.method: Two-stage encoding: 1) Cross-modal vision-text encoding generates informative images/text for temporal patterns via MLLMs; 2) ISTS encoding extracts enriched temporal features with multi-view embedding fusion and temporal-variable encoder. Adaptive query-based feature extractor compresses MLLM tokens, and multimodal alignment with modality-aware gating bridges modality gaps.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real data demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed solutions, showing improved forecasting performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: MM-ISTS successfully bridges temporal, visual, and textual modalities for ISTS forecasting, capturing intricate patterns and contextual understanding while reducing computational costs through adaptive feature extraction.

title: Restoring Linguistic Grounding in VLA Models via Train-Free Attention Recalibration
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to perform manipulation tasks directly from natural language instructions and are increasingly viewed as a foundation for generalist robotic policies. However, their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions remains underexplored. In this paper, we reveal a critical failure mode in which VLA policies continue executing visually plausible actions even when the language instruction contradicts the scene. We refer to this phenomenon as linguistic blindness, where VLA policies prioritize visual priors over instruction semantics during action generation. To systematically analyze this issue, we introduce ICBench, a diagnostic benchmark constructed from the LIBERO dataset that probes language-action coupling by injecting controlled OOD instruction contradictions while keeping the visual environment unchanged. Evaluations on three representative VLA architectures, including Pi0, Pi0.5 and OpenVLA OFT, show that these models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing a strong visual bias in action generation. To mitigate this issue, we propose Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR), a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore the influence of language instructions. IGAR operates without retraining or architectural modification and can be directly applied to existing VLA models. Experiments across 30 LIBERO tasks demonstrate that IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance. We additionally validate the approach on a real Franka robotic arm, where IGAR effectively prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.

AI.tldr: VLA models suffer from "linguistic blindness" - they execute visually plausible actions even when instructions contradict the scene. The paper introduces ICBench to diagnose this issue and proposes IGAR, a train-free inference-time mechanism to recalibrate attention toward language instructions.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are promising for generalist robotic policies but their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions is underexplored. The paper identifies a critical failure mode where VLA policies prioritize visual priors over instruction semantics, executing actions even when instructions contradict the scene.
AI.method: 1) Introduces ICBench, a diagnostic benchmark constructed from LIBERO dataset to probe language-action coupling with controlled OOD instruction contradictions. 2) Proposes Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR), a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore language instruction influence without retraining or architectural modifications.
AI.result: Evaluations on three VLA architectures (Pi0, Pi0.5, OpenVLA OFT) show models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing strong visual bias. IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance across 30 LIBERO tasks. Real-world validation on a Franka robotic arm shows IGAR effectively prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.
AI.conclusion: VLA models exhibit "linguistic blindness" where visual priors dominate instruction semantics. IGAR provides an effective, practical solution to mitigate this issue without requiring model retraining, improving reliability of VLA policies in real-world robotic applications where instruction-scene consistency is crucial.

title: Demystifying KAN for Vision Tasks: The RepKAN Approach
summary: Remote sensing image classification is essential for Earth observation, yet standard CNNs and Transformers often function as uninterpretable black-boxes. We propose RepKAN, a novel architecture that integrates the structural efficiency of CNNs with the non-linear representational power of KANs. By utilizing a dual-path design -- Spatial Linear and Spectral Non-linear -- RepKAN enables the autonomous discovery of class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds. Experimental results on the EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45 datasets demonstrate that RepKAN provides explicit physically interpretable reasoning while outperforming state-of-the-art models. These findings indicate that RepKAN holds significant potential to serve as the backbone for future interpretable visual foundation models.

AI.tldr: RepKAN is a novel interpretable architecture for remote sensing image classification that combines CNN efficiency with KAN's non-linear power, enabling autonomous discovery of spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds while outperforming SOTA models.
AI.motivation: Standard CNNs and Transformers for remote sensing image classification function as uninterpretable black-boxes, lacking explicit physical reasoning about spectral signatures and interactions in Earth observation data.
AI.method: RepKAN integrates CNN structural efficiency with KAN's non-linear representational power using a dual-path design: Spatial Linear path and Spectral Non-linear path, enabling autonomous discovery of class-specific spectral fingerprints and physical interaction manifolds.
AI.result: Experimental results on EuroSAT and NWPU-RESISC45 datasets demonstrate that RepKAN provides explicit physically interpretable reasoning while outperforming state-of-the-art models in classification performance.
AI.conclusion: RepKAN holds significant potential to serve as the backbone for future interpretable visual foundation models in remote sensing, bridging the gap between high performance and physical interpretability.

title: MASFactory: A Graph-centric Framework for Orchestrating LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems with Vibe Graphing
comment: Submitted to ACL 2026 Demo Track. 10 pages, 6 figures. Code and documentation are available at: https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory
summary: Large language model-based (LLM-based) multi-agent systems (MAS) are increasingly used to extend agentic problem solving via role specialization and collaboration. MAS workflows can be naturally modeled as directed computation graphs, where nodes execute agents/sub-workflows and edges encode dependencies and message passing. However, implementing complex graph workflows in current frameworks still requires substantial manual effort, offers limited reuse, and makes it difficult to integrate heterogeneous external context sources. To overcome these limitations, we present MASFactory, a graph-centric framework for orchestrating LLM-based MAS. It introduces Vibe Graphing, a human-in-the-loop approach that compiles natural-language intent into an editable workflow specification and then into an executable graph. In addition, the framework provides reusable components and pluggable context integration, as well as a visualizer for topology preview, runtime tracing, and human-in-the-loop interaction. We evaluate MASFactory on seven public benchmarks, validating both reproduction consistency for representative MAS methods and the effectiveness of Vibe Graphing. Our code (https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory) and video (https://youtu.be/ANynzVfY32k) are publicly available.

AI.tldr: MASFactory is a graph-centric framework for orchestrating LLM-based multi-agent systems that uses Vibe Graphing to compile natural-language intent into executable workflows with reusable components and pluggable context integration.
AI.motivation: Current frameworks for implementing complex graph workflows in LLM-based multi-agent systems require substantial manual effort, offer limited reuse, and make it difficult to integrate heterogeneous external context sources.
AI.method: MASFactory introduces Vibe Graphing, a human-in-the-loop approach that compiles natural-language intent into an editable workflow specification and then into an executable graph. The framework provides reusable components, pluggable context integration, and a visualizer for topology preview, runtime tracing, and human-in-the-loop interaction.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on seven public benchmarks, validating both reproduction consistency for representative MAS methods and the effectiveness of Vibe Graphing.
AI.conclusion: MASFactory addresses limitations in current LLM-based multi-agent system frameworks by providing a graph-centric orchestration approach with natural-language workflow specification, reusable components, and integrated visualization tools.

title: Probing Visual Concepts in Lightweight Vision-Language Models for Automated Driving
summary: The use of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in automated driving applications is becoming increasingly common, with the aim of leveraging their reasoning and generalisation capabilities to handle long tail scenarios. However, these models often fail on simple visual questions that are highly relevant to automated driving, and the reasons behind these failures remain poorly understood. In this work, we examine the intermediate activations of VLMs and assess the extent to which specific visual concepts are linearly encoded, with the goal of identifying bottlenecks in the flow of visual information. Specifically, we create counterfactual image sets that differ only in a targeted visual concept and then train linear probes to distinguish between them using the activations of four state-of-the-art (SOTA) VLMs. Our results show that concepts such as the presence of an object or agent in a scene are explicitly and linearly encoded, whereas other spatial visual concepts, such as the orientation of an object or agent, are only implicitly encoded by the spatial structure retained by the vision encoder. In parallel, we observe that in certain cases, even when a concept is linearly encoded in the model's activations, the model still fails to answer correctly. This leads us to identify two failure modes. The first is perceptual failure, where the visual information required to answer a question is not linearly encoded in the model's activations. The second is cognitive failure, where the visual information is present but the model fails to align it correctly with language semantics. Finally, we show that increasing the distance of the object in question quickly degrades the linear separability of the corresponding visual concept. Overall, our findings improve our understanding of failure cases in VLMs on simple visual tasks that are highly relevant to automated driving.

AI.tldr: VLMs fail on simple driving-relevant visual questions despite reasoning capabilities; analysis reveals perceptual vs. cognitive failures via linear probing of visual concepts.
AI.motivation: VLMs are increasingly used in automated driving for handling long-tail scenarios, but they often fail on simple visual questions relevant to driving. The reasons for these failures remain poorly understood, motivating investigation into how visual information flows through VLMs and where bottlenecks occur.
AI.method: Created counterfactual image sets differing only in targeted visual concepts, then trained linear probes to distinguish between them using activations from four SOTA VLMs. Analyzed linear separability of concepts like object presence, orientation, and spatial relationships.
AI.result: Object/agent presence is explicitly and linearly encoded, while spatial concepts like orientation are only implicitly encoded via vision encoder's spatial structure. Identified two failure modes: perceptual (visual info not linearly encoded) and cognitive (info present but misaligned with language semantics). Object distance degrades linear separability of visual concepts.
AI.conclusion: The study improves understanding of VLM failures on driving-relevant visual tasks by revealing perceptual vs. cognitive failure modes and showing that spatial concepts are less explicitly encoded than object presence. Distance effects on linear separability highlight limitations for driving applications.

title: TempoSyncDiff: Distilled Temporally-Consistent Diffusion for Low-Latency Audio-Driven Talking Head Generation
summary: Diffusion models have recently advanced photorealistic human synthesis, although practical talking-head generation (THG) remains constrained by high inference latency, temporal instability such as flicker and identity drift, and imperfect audio-visual alignment under challenging speech conditions. This paper introduces TempoSyncDiff, a reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework that explores few-step inference for efficient audio-driven talking-head generation. The approach adopts a teacher-student distillation formulation in which a diffusion teacher trained with a standard noise prediction objective guides a lightweight student denoiser capable of operating with significantly fewer inference steps to improve generation stability. The framework incorporates identity anchoring and temporal regularization designed to mitigate identity drift and frame-to-frame flicker during synthesis, while viseme-based audio conditioning provides coarse lip motion control. Experiments on the LRS3 dataset report denoising-stage component-level metrics relative to VAE reconstructions and preliminary latency characterization, including CPU-only and edge computing measurements and feasibility estimates for edge deployment. The results suggest that distilled diffusion models can retain much of the reconstruction behaviour of a stronger teacher while enabling substantially lower latency inference. The study is positioned as an initial step toward practical diffusion-based talking-head generation under constrained computational settings. GitHub: https://mazumdarsoumya.github.io/TempoSyncDiff

AI.tldr: TempoSyncDiff is a reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework for efficient audio-driven talking-head generation that uses teacher-student distillation to enable few-step inference while maintaining quality, with identity anchoring and temporal regularization to reduce flicker and identity drift.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models for photorealistic human synthesis face practical limitations in talking-head generation including high inference latency, temporal instability (flicker and identity drift), and imperfect audio-visual alignment under challenging speech conditions.
AI.method: A reference-conditioned latent diffusion framework with teacher-student distillation: a diffusion teacher trained with standard noise prediction guides a lightweight student denoiser for few-step inference. Incorporates identity anchoring and temporal regularization to mitigate identity drift and flicker, plus viseme-based audio conditioning for coarse lip motion control.
AI.result: Experiments on LRS3 dataset show distilled diffusion models retain much reconstruction behavior of stronger teacher while enabling substantially lower latency inference. Component-level metrics relative to VAE reconstructions and preliminary latency characterization including CPU-only and edge computing measurements suggest feasibility for edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates distilled diffusion models can achieve practical talking-head generation under constrained computational settings, representing an initial step toward efficient diffusion-based synthesis with reduced latency and improved stability.

title: Evaluating Austrian A-Level German Essays with Large Language Models for Automated Essay Scoring
comment: To be presented at the SAC2026 and published in its symposium proceedings
summary: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) has been explored for decades with the goal to support teachers by reducing grading workload and mitigating subjective biases. While early systems relied on handcrafted features and statistical models, recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have made it possible to evaluate student writing with unprecedented flexibility. This paper investigates the application of state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs for the grading of Austrian A-level German texts, with a particular focus on rubric-based evaluation. A dataset of 101 anonymised student exams across three text types was processed and evaluated. Four LLMs, DeepSeek-R1 32b, Qwen3 30b, Mixtral 8x7b and LLama3.3 70b, were evaluated with different contexts and prompting strategies. The LLMs were able to reach a maximum of 40.6% agreement with the human rater in the rubric-provided sub-dimensions, and only 32.8% of final grades matched the ones given by a human expert. The results indicate that even though smaller models are able to use standardised rubrics for German essay grading, they are not accurate enough to be used in a real-world grading environment.

AI.tldr: Open-weight LLMs achieve only 32.8% agreement with human experts on Austrian A-level German essay grading, insufficient for real-world deployment despite rubric-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs can effectively grade Austrian A-level German texts using rubric-based evaluation, aiming to reduce teacher workload and mitigate subjective biases in automated essay scoring.
AI.method: Processed 101 anonymized student exams across three text types, evaluated four LLMs (DeepSeek-R1 32b, Qwen3 30b, Mixtral 8x7b, LLama3.3 70b) with different contexts and prompting strategies for rubric-based grading.
AI.result: Maximum 40.6% agreement with human raters on rubric sub-dimensions, only 32.8% agreement on final grades, indicating insufficient accuracy for real-world grading despite rubric usage.
AI.conclusion: Current open-weight LLMs, while capable of using standardized rubrics for German essay grading, lack sufficient accuracy for practical deployment in real-world educational assessment environments.

title: Text-Driven Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation
summary: Talking Face Generation (TFG) strives to create realistic and emotionally expressive digital faces. While previous TFG works have mastered the creation of naturalistic facial movements, they typically express a fixed target emotion in synthetic videos and lack the ability to exhibit continuously changing and natural expressions like humans do when conveying information.
